To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes a decentralized ensembling framework based on asynchronous probability aggregation and feedback distillation. By shifting the exchange unit from model weights to class-probability vectors, our method maintains data privacy, reduces communication requirements by orders of magnitude, and improves overall accuracy. This approach enables diverse CNN designs to collaborate asynchronously, enhancing disaster image identification performance even in resource-constrained settings. Experimental tests demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms traditional individual backbones and standard federated approaches, establishing a scalable and resource-aware solution for real-time disaster response.

AI.tldr: Decentralized FL framework using probability aggregation & feedback distillation for disaster image identification, reducing communication costs while improving accuracy with heterogeneous CNNs.
AI.motivation: Emergency handling in DDSS suffers from network latency and suboptimal accuracy. Traditional FL has high communication costs and rigid synchronization requirements that don't work well with heterogeneous CNN architectures.
AI.method: Proposes decentralized ensembling framework based on asynchronous probability aggregation and feedback distillation. Shifts exchange unit from model weights to class-probability vectors, enabling diverse CNN designs to collaborate asynchronously while maintaining data privacy.
AI.result: Reduces communication requirements by orders of magnitude, improves overall accuracy, and enables disaster image identification even in resource-constrained settings. Outperforms traditional individual backbones and standard federated approaches.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a scalable and resource-aware solution for real-time disaster response that allows heterogeneous CNNs to collaborate efficiently while preserving privacy and reducing communication overhead.

title: Prompt Optimization Is a Coin Flip: Diagnosing When It Helps in Compound AI Systems
summary: Prompt optimization in compound AI systems is statistically indistinguishable from a coin flip: across 72 optimization runs on Claude Haiku (6 methods $\times$ 4 tasks $\times$ 3 repeats), 49% score below zero-shot; on Amazon Nova Lite, the failure rate is even higher. Yet on one task, all six methods improve over zero-shot by up to $+6.8$ points. What distinguishes success from failure? We investigate with 18,000 grid evaluations and 144 optimization runs, testing two assumptions behind end-to-end optimization tools like TextGrad and DSPy: (A) individual prompts are worth optimizing, and (B) agent prompts interact, requiring joint optimization. Interaction effects are never significant ($p > 0.52$, all $F < 1.0$), and optimization helps only when the task has exploitable output structure -- a format the model can produce but does not default to. We provide a two-stage diagnostic: an \$80 ANOVA pre-test for agent coupling, and a 10-minute headroom test that predicts whether optimization is worthwhile -- turning a coin flip into an informed decision.

AI.tldr: Prompt optimization in compound AI systems often fails (49% below zero-shot), but can succeed up to +6.8 points when tasks have exploitable output structure; interaction effects between agent prompts are insignificant, and diagnostic tests can predict optimization value.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates why prompt optimization in compound AI systems is so unreliable, with many optimization attempts performing worse than zero-shot baselines, despite occasional significant improvements. The authors aim to understand what distinguishes successful optimization from failure and test core assumptions behind end-to-end optimization tools.
AI.method: Conducted large-scale empirical study with 72 optimization runs on Claude Haiku and additional runs on Amazon Nova Lite, plus 18,000 grid evaluations and 144 optimization runs. Tested six optimization methods across four tasks with three repeats. Used statistical analysis (ANOVA) to test assumptions about prompt interactions and task structure effects.
AI.result: Prompt optimization often fails (49% below zero-shot on Claude Haiku, worse on Amazon Nova Lite). Interaction effects between agent prompts are never significant (p > 0.52). Optimization only helps when tasks have "exploitable output structure" - formats models can produce but don't default to. Success varies dramatically by task: one task showed +6.8 point improvements across all methods.
AI.conclusion: The key to successful prompt optimization is task structure, not agent interactions. Authors propose a two-stage diagnostic: an $80 ANOVA pre-test for agent coupling, and a 10-minute headroom test that predicts whether optimization will be beneficial, transforming optimization from a coin flip into an informed decision.

title: Auxiliary Finite-Difference Residual-Gradient Regularization for PINNs
summary:
Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) are often selected by a single scalar loss even when the quantity of interest is more specific. We study a hybrid design in which the governing PDE residual remains automatic-differentiation (AD) based, while finite differences (FD) appear only in a weak auxiliary term that penalizes gradients of the sampled residual field. The FD term regularizes the residual field without replacing the PDE residual itself.
  We examine this idea in two stages. Stage 1 is a controlled Poisson benchmark comparing a baseline PINN, the FD residual-gradient regularizer, and a matched AD residual-gradient baseline. Stage 2 transfers the same logic to a three-dimensional annular heat-conduction benchmark (PINN3D), where baseline errors concentrate near a wavy outer wall and the auxiliary grid is implemented as a body-fitted shell adjacent to the wall.
  In Stage 1, the FD regularizer reproduces the main effect of residual-gradient control while exposing a trade-off between field accuracy and residual cleanliness. In Stage 2, the shell regularizer improves the application-facing quantities, namely outer-wall flux and boundary-condition behavior. Across seeds 0-5 and 100k epochs, the most reliable tested configuration is a fixed shell weight of 5e-4 under the Kourkoutas-beta optimizer regime: relative to a matched run without the shell term, it reduces the mean outer-wall BC RMSE from 1.22e-2 to 9.29e-4 and the mean wall-flux RMSE from 9.21e-3 to 9.63e-4. Adam with beta2=0.999 becomes usable when the initial learning rate is reduced to 1e-3, although its shell benefit is less robust than under Kourkoutas-beta. Overall, the results support a targeted view of hybrid PINNs: an auxiliary-only FD regularizer is most valuable when it is aligned with the physical quantity of interest, here the outer-wall flux.

AI.tldr: Hybrid PINNs using FD only in weak auxiliary term to regularize residual gradients, improving boundary quantities without replacing main AD-based PDE residual.
AI.motivation: PINNs often use single scalar loss that may not capture specific quantities of interest; need targeted regularization for specific physical quantities like boundary fluxes.
AI.method: Two-stage design: 1) Poisson benchmark comparing baseline PINN, FD residual-gradient regularizer, and matched AD baseline; 2) 3D annular heat conduction with body-fitted shell regularizer near wavy outer wall to target boundary quantities.
AI.result: FD regularizer reproduces residual-gradient control effects; shell regularizer improves outer-wall flux and BC behavior; best configuration (shell weight 5e-4, Kourkoutas-beta) reduces mean outer-wall BC RMSE from 1.22e-2 to 9.29e-4 and wall-flux RMSE from 9.21e-3 to 9.63e-4.
AI.conclusion: Targeted hybrid PINNs with auxiliary-only FD regularizers are most valuable when aligned with specific physical quantities of interest; they effectively improve boundary-condition behavior and flux accuracy without replacing the main PDE residual.

title: GDPR Auto-Formalization with AI Agents and Human Verification
summary: We study the overall process of automatic formalization of GDPR provisions using large language models, within a human-in-the-loop verification framework. Rather than aiming for full autonomy, we adopt a role-specialized workflow in which LLM-based AI components, operating in a multi-agent setting with iterative feedback, generate legal scenarios, formal rules, and atomic facts. This is coupled with independent verification modules which include human reviewers' assessment of representational, logical, and legal correctness. Using this approach, we construct a high-quality dataset to be used for GDPR auto-formalization, and analyze both successful and problematic cases. Our results show that structured verification and targeted human oversight are essential for reliable legal formalization, especially in the presence of legal nuance and context-sensitive reasoning.

AI.tldr: A human-in-the-loop verification framework using LLMs in multi-agent settings for GDPR formalization, emphasizing structured verification and human oversight over full autonomy.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of automatic formalization of GDPR provisions, recognizing that full autonomy is insufficient for legal contexts requiring nuance and context-sensitive reasoning. There's a need for reliable legal formalization that maintains representational, logical, and legal correctness.
AI.method: The method employs a role-specialized workflow with LLM-based AI components operating in a multi-agent setting with iterative feedback. The system generates legal scenarios, formal rules, and atomic facts, coupled with independent verification modules that include human reviewers assessing representational, logical, and legal correctness.
AI.result: The approach successfully constructs a high-quality dataset for GDPR auto-formalization. Analysis reveals both successful and problematic cases, demonstrating that structured verification and targeted human oversight are essential for reliable legal formalization, particularly for legal nuance and context-sensitive reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Structured verification processes and targeted human oversight are crucial for reliable automatic formalization of legal provisions like GDPR, rather than aiming for full autonomy. Human-in-the-loop frameworks with specialized roles and multi-agent LLM systems can produce high-quality legal formalizations when properly verified.

title: Scouting By Reward: VLM-TO-IRL-Driven Player Selection For Esports
summary: Traditional esports scouting workflows rely heavily on manual video review and aggregate performance metrics, which often fail to capture the nuanced decision-making patterns necessary to determine if a prospect fits a specific tactical archetype. To address this, we reframe style-based player evaluation in esports as an Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) problem. In this paper, we introduce a novel player selection framework that learns professional-specific reward functions from logged gameplay demonstrations, allowing organizations to rank candidates by their stylistic alignment with a target star player. Our proposed architecture utilizes a multimodal, two-branch intake: one branch encodes structured state-action trajectories derived from high-resolution in-game telemetry, while the second encodes temporally aligned tactical pseudo-commentary generated by Vision-Language Models (VLMs) from broadcast footage. These representations are fused and evaluated via a Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning (GAIL) objective, where a discriminator learns to capture the unique mechanical and tactical signatures of elite professionals. By transitioning from generic skill estimation to scouting "by reward," this framework provides a scalable, workflow-aware digital twin system that enables data-driven roster construction and targeted talent discovery across massive candidate pools.

AI.tldr: A novel player scouting framework for esports that uses Inverse Reinforcement Learning to rank candidates by how closely their playing style matches a target professional player, combining game telemetry with AI-generated commentary analysis.
AI.motivation: Traditional esports scouting relies on manual video review and basic performance metrics, which fail to capture nuanced decision-making patterns and tactical style needed to determine if prospects fit specific team archetypes.
AI.method: The framework treats player evaluation as an Inverse Reinforcement Learning problem, learning professional-specific reward functions from gameplay demonstrations. It uses a multimodal two-branch architecture: one branch encodes state-action trajectories from in-game telemetry, while the second encodes tactical pseudo-commentary generated by Vision-Language Models from broadcast footage. These are fused and evaluated via Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning (GAIL) where a discriminator captures mechanical and tactical signatures.
AI.result: Creates a scalable, workflow-aware digital twin system that transitions from generic skill estimation to scouting "by reward," enabling data-driven roster construction and targeted talent discovery across large candidate pools by ranking candidates based on stylistic alignment with target professional players.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a more sophisticated approach to esports talent evaluation that moves beyond basic metrics to capture nuanced decision-making patterns, offering organizations a systematic way to find players who match specific tactical archetypes and team needs.

title: El Agente Forjador: Task-Driven Agent Generation for Quantum Simulation
summary: AI for science promises to accelerate the discovery process. The advent of large language models (LLMs) and agentic workflows enables the expediting of a growing range of scientific tasks. However, most of the current generation of agentic systems depend on static, hand-curated toolsets that hinder adaptation to new domains and evolving libraries. We present El Agente Forjador, a multi-agent framework in which universal coding agents autonomously forge, validate, and reuse computational tools through a four-stage workflow of tool analysis, tool generation, task execution, and iterative solution evaluation. Evaluated across 24 tasks spanning quantum chemistry and quantum dynamics on five coding agent setups, we compare three operating modes: zero-shot generation of tools per task, reuse of a curriculum-built toolset, and direct problem-solving with the coding agents as the baseline. We find that our tool generation and reuse framework consistently improves accuracy over the baseline. We also show that reusing a toolset built by a stronger coding agent can reduce API cost and substantially raises the solution quality for weaker coding agents. Case studies further demonstrate that tools forged for different domains can be combined to solve hybrid tasks. Taken together, these results show that LLM-based agents can use their scientific knowledge and coding capabilities to autonomously build reusable scientific tools, pointing toward a paradigm in which agent capabilities are defined by the tasks they are designed to solve rather than by explicitly engineered implementations.

AI.tldr: LLM agents can autonomously create and reuse computational tools for science, outperforming static tool systems and improving solution quality across quantum chemistry and dynamics tasks.
AI.motivation: Current AI agent systems for science rely on static, hand-curated toolsets that hinder adaptation to new domains and evolving libraries. There's a need for more flexible systems that can autonomously create and adapt tools for scientific discovery.
AI.method: Developed El Agente Forjador, a multi-agent framework with universal coding agents that autonomously forge, validate, and reuse tools through a four-stage workflow: tool analysis, tool generation, task execution, and iterative solution evaluation. Tested across 24 quantum chemistry/dynamics tasks with three operating modes (zero-shot tool generation per task, reuse of curriculum-built toolsets, and direct problem-solving baseline).
AI.result: The tool generation and reuse framework consistently improves accuracy over baseline approaches. Reusing tools built by stronger coding agents reduces API costs and significantly raises solution quality for weaker agents. Tools forged for different domains can be combined to solve hybrid tasks.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based agents can leverage their scientific knowledge and coding capabilities to autonomously build reusable scientific tools, moving toward a paradigm where agent capabilities are defined by tasks rather than explicitly engineered implementations.

title: Quantization of Spiking Neural Networks Beyond Accuracy
summary: Quantization is a natural complement to the sparse, event-driven computation of Spiking Neural Networks, reducing memory bandwidth and arithmetic cost for deployment on resource-constrained hardware. However, existing SNN quantization evaluation focuses almost exclusively on accuracy, overlooking whether a quantized network preserves the firing behavior of its full-precision counterpart. We demonstrate that quantization method, clipping range, and bit-width can produce substantially different firing distributions at equivalent accuracy, differences invisible to standard metrics but relevant to deployment, where firing activity governs effective sparsity, state storage, and event-processing load. To capture this gap, we propose Earth Mover's Distance as a diagnostic metric for firing distribution divergence, and apply it systematically across weight and membrane quantization on SEW-ResNet architectures trained on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. We find that uniform quantization induces distributional drift even when accuracy is preserved, while LQ-Net style learned quantization maintains firing behavior close to the full-precision baseline. Our results suggest that behavior preservation should be treated as an evaluation criterion alongside accuracy, and that EMD provides a principled tool for assessing it.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Earth Mover's Distance as a new metric to evaluate SNN quantization beyond just accuracy, showing that different quantization methods affect firing behavior significantly even when accuracy appears preserved.
AI.motivation: Current SNN quantization evaluation focuses only on accuracy, ignoring whether quantized networks preserve the firing behavior of full-precision counterparts. This gap is important because firing activity governs sparsity, state storage, and event-processing load in deployment.
AI.method: Proposed Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) as a diagnostic metric for firing distribution divergence. Systematically applied EMD across weight and membrane quantization on SEW-ResNet architectures trained on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, comparing uniform quantization with LQ-Net style learned quantization.
AI.result: Uniform quantization induces substantial distributional drift in firing behavior even when accuracy is preserved, while LQ-Net style learned quantization maintains firing behavior close to full-precision baselines. The differences in firing distributions are invisible to standard accuracy metrics but relevant to deployment.
AI.conclusion: Behavior preservation should be treated as an evaluation criterion alongside accuracy for SNN quantization, and Earth Mover's Distance provides a principled tool for assessing firing behavior preservation during quantization.

title: CoDaS: AI Co-Data-Scientist for Biomarker Discovery via Wearable Sensors
summary: Scientific discovery in digital health requires converting continuous physiological signals from wearable devices into clinically actionable biomarkers. We introduce CoDaS (AI Co-Data-Scientist), a multi-agent system that structures biomarker discovery as an iterative process combining hypothesis generation, statistical analysis, adversarial validation, and literature-grounded reasoning with human oversight using large-scale wearable datasets. Across three cohorts totaling 9,279 participant-observations, CoDaS identified 41 candidate digital biomarkers for mental health and 25 for metabolic outcomes, each subjected to an internal validation battery spanning replication, stability, robustness, and discriminative power. Across two independent depression cohorts, CoDaS surfaced circadian instability-related features in both datasets, reflected in sleep duration variability (DWB, ρ= 0.252, p < 0.001) and sleep onset variability (GLOBEM, ρ= 0.126, p < 0.001). In a metabolic cohort, CoDaS derived a cardiovascular fitness index (steps/resting heart rate; ρ= -0.374, p < 0.001), and recovered established clinical associations, including the hepatic function ratio (AST/ALT; ρ= -0.375, p < 0.001), a known correlate of insulin resistance. Incorporating CoDaS-derived features alongside demographic variables led to modest but consistent improvements in predictive performance, with cross-validated ΔR^2 increases of 0.040 for depression and 0.021 for insulin resistance. These findings suggest that CoDaS enables systematic and traceable hypothesis generation and prioritization for biomarker discovery from large-scale wearable data.

AI.tldr: CoDaS is an AI multi-agent system that systematically discovers digital biomarkers from wearable data through iterative hypothesis generation, validation, and literature reasoning.
AI.motivation: There's a need to convert continuous physiological signals from wearable devices into clinically actionable biomarkers for scientific discovery in digital health. Current approaches lack systematic, scalable methods for biomarker discovery from large-scale wearable datasets.
AI.method: CoDaS structures biomarker discovery as an iterative process combining: hypothesis generation, statistical analysis, adversarial validation, and literature-grounded reasoning with human oversight. The system was tested across three cohorts totaling 9,279 participant-observations.
AI.result: CoDaS identified 41 candidate digital biomarkers for mental health and 25 for metabolic outcomes, with validation across replication, stability, robustness, and discriminative power. Key findings include: circadian instability features correlated with depression (sleep variability ρ=0.252-0.126, p<0.001), cardiovascular fitness index (steps/RHR ρ=-0.374, p<0.001), and hepatic function ratio recovery (AST/ALT ρ=-0.375, p<0.001). Incorporating CoDaS-derived features improved predictive performance (ΔR²: +0.040 for depression, +0.021 for insulin resistance).
AI.conclusion: CoDaS enables systematic, traceable hypothesis generation and prioritization for biomarker discovery from large-scale wearable data, demonstrating feasibility in identifying clinically relevant digital biomarkers for mental health and metabolic outcomes.

title: On the Expressive Power and Limitations of Multi-Layer SSMs
summary: We study the expressive power and limitations of multi-layer state-space models (SSMs). First, we show that multi-layer SSMs face fundamental limitations in compositional tasks, revealing an inherent gap between SSMs and streaming models. Then, we examine the role of chain-of-thought (CoT), showing that offline CoT does not fundamentally increase the expressiveness, while online CoT can substantially increase its power. Indeed, with online CoT, multi-layer SSMs become equivalent in power to streaming algorithms. Finally, we investigate the tradeoff between width and precision, showing that these resources are not interchangeable in the base model, but admit a clean equivalence once online CoT is allowed. Overall, our results offer a unified perspective on how depth, finite precision, and CoT shape the power and limits of SSMs.

AI.tldr: Multi-layer SSMs have fundamental limitations in compositional tasks, but online CoT makes them equivalent to streaming algorithms, while offline CoT does not. Width and precision aren't interchangeable in base models but become equivalent with online CoT.
AI.motivation: To understand the expressive power and limitations of multi-layer state-space models (SSMs), particularly how they compare to streaming models, and how chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning and architectural resources affect their computational capabilities.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of multi-layer SSMs: 1) Showing fundamental limitations in compositional tasks revealing gaps with streaming models; 2) Examining different CoT approaches (online vs offline); 3) Investigating tradeoffs between width and precision in the architecture.
AI.result: 1) Multi-layer SSMs have inherent limitations in compositional tasks creating a gap with streaming models. 2) Online CoT substantially increases SSM power making them equivalent to streaming algorithms, while offline CoT does not fundamentally increase expressiveness. 3) Width and precision are not interchangeable in base models but admit clean equivalence with online CoT.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a unified perspective showing how depth, finite precision, and CoT shape SSM capabilities, establishing that online CoT is key to overcoming fundamental limitations and achieving streaming algorithm equivalence.

title: A Parallel Approach to Counting Exact Covers Based on Decomposability Property
summary: The exact cover problem is a classical NP-hard problem with broad applications in the area of AI. Algorithm DXZ is a method to count exact covers representing by zero-suppressed binary decision diagrams (ZBDDs). In this paper, we propose a zero-suppressed variant of decision decomposable negation normal form (in short, decision-ZDNNF), which is strictly more succinct than ZBDDs. We then design a novel parallel algorithm, namely DXD, which constructs a decision-ZDNNF representing the set of all exact covers. Furthermore, we improve DXD by dynamically updating connected components. The experimental results demonstrate that the improved DXD algorithm outperforms all of state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: DXD algorithm improves exact cover counting using zero-suppressed decision DNNF, outperforming state-of-the-art methods including DXZ
AI.motivation: The exact cover problem is NP-hard and important for AI applications. Existing method DXZ uses ZBDDs, but there's room for improvement with more succinct representations.
AI.method: Proposed decision-ZDNNF (zero-suppressed decision decomposable negation normal form) that's more succinct than ZBDDs, then designed parallel algorithm DXD to construct decision-ZDNNF representing all exact covers, with further improvement through dynamic connected component updates.
AI.result: Experimental results show the improved DXD algorithm outperforms all state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Decision-ZDNNF provides a more efficient representation than ZBDDs for exact cover problems, and the DXD algorithm with dynamic connected component optimization achieves superior performance.

title: CI-CBM: Class-Incremental Concept Bottleneck Model for Interpretable Continual Learning
comment: 31 pages, 6 figures. Published in Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR), 04/2026
summary: Catastrophic forgetting remains a fundamental challenge in continual learning, in which models often forget previous knowledge when fine-tuned on a new task. This issue is especially pronounced in class incremental learning (CIL), which is the most challenging setting in continual learning. Existing methods to address catastrophic forgetting often sacrifice either model interpretability or accuracy. To address this challenge, we introduce ClassIncremental Concept Bottleneck Model (CI-CBM), which leverage effective techniques, including concept regularization and pseudo-concept generation to maintain interpretable decision processes throughout incremental learning phases. Through extensive evaluation on seven datasets, CI-CBM achieves comparable performance to black-box models and outperforms previous interpretable approaches in CIL, with an average 36% accuracy gain. CICBM provides interpretable decisions on individual inputs and understandable global decision rules, as shown in our experiments, thereby demonstrating that human understandable concepts can be maintained during incremental learning without compromising model performance. Our approach is effective in both pretrained and non-pretrained scenarios; in the latter, the backbone is trained from scratch during the first learning phase. Code is publicly available at github.com/importAmir/CI-CBM.

AI.tldr: CI-CBM is an interpretable continual learning model that prevents catastrophic forgetting in class incremental learning while maintaining human-understandable concepts, achieving performance comparable to black-box models.
AI.motivation: Catastrophic forgetting is a fundamental challenge in continual learning, especially in class incremental learning (CIL). Existing methods often sacrifice either model interpretability or accuracy, creating a need for solutions that maintain both throughout incremental learning phases.
AI.method: Introduces ClassIncremental Concept Bottleneck Model (CI-CBM) using concept regularization and pseudo-concept generation techniques to maintain interpretable decision processes during incremental learning. Works in both pretrained and non-pretrained scenarios.
AI.result: Achieves comparable performance to black-box models and outperforms previous interpretable approaches in CIL with an average 36% accuracy gain across seven datasets. Provides interpretable decisions on individual inputs and understandable global decision rules.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that human-understandable concepts can be maintained during incremental learning without compromising model performance, offering an effective solution to catastrophic forgetting in CIL while preserving interpretability.

title: Learning to Draw ASCII Improves Spatial Reasoning in Language Models
summary: When faced with complex spatial problems, humans naturally sketch layouts to organize their thinking, and the act of drawing further sharpens their understanding. In this work, we ask whether a similar principle holds for Large Language Models (LLMs): can learning to construct explicit visual layouts from spatial descriptions instill genuine spatial understanding? We introduce Text2Space, a dataset that pairs natural language descriptions with ground-truth ASCII grid layouts and spatial QA pairs, enabling us to separate failures in constructing spatial representations from failures in reasoning over them. We adopt ASCII because it is human-readable, operates entirely within the token space of language models, and encodes spatial relations in a structurally verifiable form. Our evaluation reveals a pronounced "Read-Write Asymmetry": LLMs interpret ASCII representations effectively but struggle to produce them from text, and these construction errors propagate to incorrect answers downstream. To address this limitation, we train models on layout construction (Text$\rightarrow$ASCII) and find that it significantly improves spatial reasoning from text alone, even without producing any ASCII at inference time. Combining construction with comprehension training further amplifies these gains. Crucially, these improvements transfer to three external spatial reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating that, much as sketching sharpens human spatial thinking, learning to construct explicit layouts instills spatial understanding that generalizes beyond the training format.

AI.tldr: LLMs struggle to generate explicit ASCII spatial layouts from text descriptions but perform better at interpreting them, and training on layout construction improves spatial reasoning capabilities even without producing layouts at inference time.
AI.motivation: To explore whether learning to construct explicit visual layouts from spatial descriptions can instill genuine spatial understanding in LLMs, similar to how sketching helps humans organize and sharpen spatial thinking.
AI.method: Introduced Text2Space dataset with natural language descriptions paired with ASCII grid layouts and spatial QA pairs. Evaluated LLMs on layout interpretation vs. construction, then trained models on Text→ASCII layout construction and comprehension tasks.
AI.result: Revealed "Read-Write Asymmetry" - LLMs interpret ASCII representations well but struggle to produce them from text. Training on layout construction significantly improves spatial reasoning from text alone, and combining construction with comprehension training amplifies gains. Improvements transfer to external spatial reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Learning to construct explicit spatial layouts instills spatial understanding in LLMs that generalizes beyond training format, mirroring how sketching sharpens human spatial thinking. ASCII layouts serve as effective intermediate representations for spatial reasoning.

title: CSRA: Controlled Spectral Residual Augmentation for Robust Sepsis Prediction
summary: Accurate prediction of future risk and disease progression in sepsis is clinically important for early warning and timely intervention in intensive care. However, short-window sepsis prediction remains challenging, because shorter observation windows provide limited historical evidence, whereas longer prediction horizons reduce the number of patient trajectories with valid future supervision. To address this problem, we propose CSRA, a Controlled Spectral Residual Augmentation framework for short-window multi-system ICU time series. CSRA first groups variables by clinical systems and extracts system-level and global representations. It then performs input-adaptive residual perturbation in the spectral domain to generate structured and clinically plausible trajectory variations. To improve augmentation stability and controllability, CSRA is trained end-to-end with the downstream predictor under a unified objective, together with anchor consistency loss and controller regularization. Experiments on a MIMIC-IV sepsis cohort across multiple downstream models show that CSRA is consistently competitive and often superior, reducing regression error by 10.2\% in MSE and 3.7\% in MAE over the non-augmentation baseline, while also yielding consistent gains on classification. CSRA further maintains more favorable performance under shorter observation windows, longer prediction horizons, and smaller training data scales, while also remaining effective on an external clinical dataset~(ZiGongICUinfection), indicating stronger robustness and generalizability in clinically constrained settings.

AI.tldr: CSRA: A controlled spectral residual augmentation framework that improves short-window sepsis prediction in ICU by generating clinically plausible trajectory variations through input-adaptive residual perturbation in the spectral domain.
AI.motivation: Short-window sepsis prediction is challenging because shorter observation windows provide limited historical evidence, while longer prediction horizons reduce the number of patient trajectories with valid future supervision, creating a data scarcity problem for early warning and timely intervention in ICU.
AI.method: CSRA groups variables by clinical systems, extracts system-level and global representations, performs input-adaptive residual perturbation in the spectral domain to generate structured and clinically plausible trajectory variations, and trains end-to-end with downstream predictor using anchor consistency loss and controller regularization for stability and controllability.
AI.result: Experiments on MIMIC-IV sepsis cohort show CSRA reduces regression error by 10.2% in MSE and 3.7% in MAE over non-augmentation baselines, with consistent gains on classification. It maintains better performance under shorter observation windows, longer prediction horizons, and smaller training data scales, and shows effectiveness on external ZiGongICUinfection dataset.
AI.conclusion: CSRA demonstrates stronger robustness and generalizability in clinically constrained settings for short-window sepsis prediction, making it a promising approach for early warning and timely intervention in ICU care.

title: Targeted Exploration via Unified Entropy Control for Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted for publication in Findings of the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: Recent advances in reinforcement learning (RL) have improved the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs). However, the widely used Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) consistently suffers from entropy collapse, causing the policy to converge prematurely and lose diversity. Existing exploration methods introduce additional bias or variance during exploration, making it difficult to maintain optimization stability. We propose Unified Entropy Control for Reinforcement Learning (UEC-RL), a framework that provides targeted mechanisms for exploration and stabilization. UEC-RL activates more exploration on difficult prompts to search for potential and valuable reasoning trajectories. In parallel, a stabilizer prevents entropy from growing uncontrollably, thereby keeping training stable as the model consolidates reliable behaviors. Together, these components expand the search space when needed while maintaining robust optimization throughout training. Experiments on both LLM and VLM reasoning tasks show consistent gains over RL baselines on both Pass@1 and Pass@$k$. On Geometry3K, UEC-RL achieves a 37.9\% relative improvement over GRPO, indicating that it sustains effective exploration without compromising convergence and underscoring UEC-RL as a key for scaling RL-based reasoning in large models. Our code is available at https://github.com/597358816/UEC-RL.

AI.tldr: UEC-RL addresses entropy collapse in RL for large models by providing unified exploration and stabilization mechanisms to maintain diversity and stable training.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the problem of entropy collapse in GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) used for RL-based reasoning in large language models and vision-language models. This entropy collapse causes premature convergence and loss of policy diversity, while existing exploration methods introduce bias or variance that compromises optimization stability.
AI.method: The authors propose Unified Entropy Control for Reinforcement Learning (UEC-RL), a framework with two key components: 1) An exploration mechanism that activates more exploration on difficult prompts to search for valuable reasoning trajectories, and 2) A stabilizer that prevents entropy from growing uncontrollably to maintain training stability as the model consolidates reliable behaviors.
AI.result: Experiments on LLM and VLM reasoning tasks show consistent improvements over RL baselines on both Pass@1 and Pass@k metrics. On Geometry3K, UEC-RL achieves a 37.9% relative improvement over GRPO. The method sustains effective exploration without compromising convergence.
AI.conclusion: UEC-RL effectively solves the entropy collapse problem in RL for large models, enabling both expanded search space when needed and robust optimization throughout training. The results position UEC-RL as a key framework for scaling RL-based reasoning in large models while maintaining diversity and stability.

title: An unsupervised decision-support framework for multivariate biomarker analysis in athlete monitoring
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables, submitted to Springer Nature Scientific Reports
summary: Purpose. Athlete monitoring is constrained by small cohorts, heterogeneous biomarker scales, limited feasibility of repeated sampling, and the lack of reliable injury ground truth. These limitations reduce the interpretability and utility of traditional univariate and binary risk models. This study addresses these challenges by proposing an unsupervised multivariate framework to identify latent physiological states in athletes using real data. Methods. We propose a modular computational framework that operates in the joint biomarker space, integrating preprocessing, clinical safety screening, unsupervised clustering, and centroid-based physiological interpretation. Profiles are learned exclusively from amateur soccer players during a competitive microcycle. Synthetic data augmentation evaluates robustness and scalability. Ward hierarchical clustering supports monitoring and etiological differentiation, while Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) enable structural stability analysis in high-dimensional settings. Results. The framework identifies coherent profiles that distinguish mechanical damage from metabolic stress while preserving homeostatic states. Synthetic data augmentation demonstrates feasibility and detection of latent silent risk phenotypes typically missed by univariate monitoring. Structural analyses indicate robustness under augmentation and higher-dimensional settings. Conclusion. The framework enables interpretable identification of latent physiological states from multivariate biomarker data without injury labels. By distinguishing mechanisms and revealing silent risk patterns not captured by conventional monitoring, it provides actionable insights for individualized athlete monitoring and decision making.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised multivariate framework identifies latent physiological states in athletes using biomarker data without injury labels, distinguishing mechanical vs. metabolic stress and revealing silent risk patterns missed by traditional monitoring.
AI.motivation: Traditional athlete monitoring faces limitations: small cohorts, heterogeneous biomarker scales, limited repeated sampling feasibility, and lack of reliable injury ground truth. These constraints reduce interpretability and utility of conventional univariate and binary risk models.
AI.method: Modular computational framework operating in joint biomarker space with: preprocessing, clinical safety screening, unsupervised clustering, centroid-based physiological interpretation. Uses amateur soccer player data from competitive microcycle. Synthetic data augmentation for robustness evaluation. Combines Ward hierarchical clustering for monitoring/etiological differentiation and Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) for structural stability analysis in high dimensions.
AI.result: Framework identifies coherent profiles distinguishing mechanical damage from metabolic stress while preserving homeostatic states. Synthetic augmentation shows feasibility and detection of latent silent risk phenotypes typically missed by univariate monitoring. Structural analyses indicate robustness under augmentation and higher-dimensional settings.
AI.conclusion: The unsupervised multivariate framework enables interpretable identification of latent physiological states without injury labels, distinguishing mechanisms and revealing silent risk patterns not captured by conventional monitoring, providing actionable insights for individualized athlete monitoring and decision making.

title: AgentGA: Evolving Code Solutions in Agent-Seed Space
summary: We present AgentGA, a framework that evolves autonomous code-generation runs by optimizing the agent seed: the task prompt plus optional parent archives that initialize a fresh workspace. The outer loop searches over these reusable starting conditions rather than editing code directly. Each generation launches a fresh autonomous run from a reset workspace, while selected parent archives provide inherited artifacts that descendants can inspect and reuse. AgentGA couples a population-level genetic algorithm with long-horizon agents; selection uses deterministic 1:1 elite tournaments and operator allocation is adapted online with a modified Hedge controller. We instantiate the approach for tabular AutoML on the 16-competition Weco-Kaggle Lite benchmark. On the 10 benchmark runs reported here, AgentGA averages 74.52% Exceeds % of Human versus 54.15% for AIDE. Across 1135 parent-child comparisons, descendants given parent archives outperform runs started from scratch, indicating that inherited artifacts improve later autonomous runs. These findings support agent-seed optimization as a practical design point for autonomous code-search systems.

AI.tldr: AgentGA is a framework that evolves autonomous code-generation by optimizing agent seeds (task prompts + parent archives) rather than directly editing code, using genetic algorithms with long-horizon agents, achieving superior performance on tabular AutoML benchmarks compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous code-generation systems often operate from scratch each time, missing opportunities to reuse valuable artifacts from previous runs. There's a need for methods that can systematically optimize the starting conditions for autonomous agents to improve performance and efficiency.
AI.method: AgentGA uses a two-level approach: an outer genetic algorithm searches over agent seeds (task prompts + parent archives) while each generation launches fresh autonomous runs from reset workspaces. The framework combines population-level genetic algorithms with long-horizon agents, using deterministic 1:1 elite tournaments for selection and a modified Hedge controller for adaptive operator allocation.
AI.result: On the 16-competition Weco-Kaggle Lite benchmark, AgentGA averages 74.52% Exceeds % of Human versus 54.15% for AIDE. Across 1135 parent-child comparisons, descendants with parent archives consistently outperform runs started from scratch, demonstrating the value of inherited artifacts.
AI.conclusion: Agent-seed optimization is a practical design point for autonomous code-search systems, where optimizing reusable starting conditions (rather than direct code editing) significantly improves performance by enabling inheritance and reuse of valuable artifacts across generations.

title: Predicting Post-Traumatic Epilepsy from Clinical Records using Large Language Model Embeddings
summary: Objective: Post-traumatic epilepsy (PTE) is a debilitating neurological disorder that develops after traumatic brain injury (TBI). Early prediction of PTE remains challenging due to heterogeneous clinical data, limited positive cases, and reliance on resource-intensive neuroimaging data. We investigate whether routinely collected acute clinical records alone can support early PTE prediction using language model-based approaches. Methods: Using a curated subset of the TRACK-TBI cohort, we developed an automated PTE prediction framework that implements pretrained large language models (LLMs) as fixed feature extractors to encode clinical records. Tabular features, LLM-generated embeddings, and hybrid feature representations were evaluated using gradient-boosted tree classifiers under stratified cross-validation. Results: LLM embeddings achieved performance improvements by capturing contextual clinical information compared to using tabular features alone. The best performance was achieved by a modality-aware feature fusion strategy combining tabular features and LLM embeddings, achieving an AUC-ROC of 0.892 and AUPRC of 0.798. Acute post-traumatic seizures, injury severity, neurosurgical intervention, and ICU stay are key contributors to the predictive performance. Significance: These findings demonstrate that routine acute clinical records contain information suitable for early PTE risk prediction using LLM embeddings in conjunction with gradient-boosted tree classifiers. This approach represents a promising complement to imaging-based prediction.

AI.tldr: LLM-based framework using routine clinical records achieves accurate early prediction of post-traumatic epilepsy after TBI, outperforming traditional tabular approaches.
AI.motivation: Post-traumatic epilepsy (PTE) is debilitating but early prediction is challenging due to heterogeneous clinical data, limited positive cases, and reliance on resource-intensive neuroimaging.
AI.method: Used TRACK-TBI cohort data to develop automated PTE prediction framework with pretrained LLMs as fixed feature extractors to encode clinical records. Evaluated tabular features, LLM embeddings, and hybrid representations with gradient-boosted tree classifiers under stratified cross-validation.
AI.result: LLM embeddings captured contextual clinical information better than tabular features alone. Best performance came from modality-aware feature fusion combining tabular features and LLM embeddings (AUC-ROC: 0.892, AUPRC: 0.798). Key predictors: acute post-traumatic seizures, injury severity, neurosurgical intervention, and ICU stay.
AI.conclusion: Routine acute clinical records contain sufficient information for early PTE risk prediction using LLM embeddings with gradient-boosted trees, representing a promising complement to imaging-based approaches.

title: Rethinking Patient Education as Multi-turn Multi-modal Interaction
comment: Equal contribution for the first two authors
summary: Most medical multimodal benchmarks focus on static tasks such as image question answering, report generation, and plain-language rewriting. Patient education is more demanding: systems must identify relevant evidence across images, show patients where to look, explain findings in accessible language, and handle confusion or distress. Yet most patient education work remains text-only, even though combined image-and-text explanations may better support understanding. We introduce MedImageEdu, a benchmark for multi-turn, evidence-grounded radiology patient education. Each case provides a radiology report with report text and case images. A DoctorAgent interacts with a PatientAgent, conditioned on a hidden profile that captures factors such as education level, health literacy, and personality. When a patient question would benefit from visual support, the DoctorAgent can issue drawing instructions grounded in the report, case images, and the current question to a benchmark-provided drawing tool. The tool returns image(s), after which the DoctorAgent produces a final multimodal response consisting of the image(s) and a grounded plain-language explanation. MedImageEdu contains 150 cases from three sources and evaluates both the consultation process and the final multimodal response along five dimensions: Consultation, Safety and Scope, Language Quality, Drawing Quality, and Image-Text Response Quality. Across representative open- and closed-source vision-language model agents, we find three consistent gaps: fluent language often outpaces faithful visual grounding, safety is the weakest dimension across disease categories, and emotionally tense interactions are harder than low education or low health literacy. MedImageEdu provides a controlled testbed for assessing whether multimodal agents can teach from evidence rather than merely answer from text.

AI.tldr: MedImageEdu: A benchmark for multi-turn, evidence-grounded radiology patient education that evaluates multimodal agents' ability to combine images and text for effective patient teaching, rather than just answering questions.
AI.motivation: Current medical benchmarks focus on static tasks like image QA or report generation, but patient education requires dynamic interaction involving visual evidence, clear explanations, and emotional support. Most patient education work remains text-only, missing the benefits of combined image-text explanations for better patient understanding.
AI.method: Created MedImageEdu benchmark with 150 radiology cases (report + images). Uses DoctorAgent interacting with PatientAgent having hidden profiles (education level, health literacy, personality). DoctorAgent can issue drawing instructions for visual support, then produce multimodal responses (images + grounded explanations). Evaluates consultation process and final responses across five dimensions.
AI.result: Benchmark evaluation revealed three consistent gaps: 1) fluent language often outpaces faithful visual grounding, 2) safety is weakest dimension across disease categories, and 3) emotionally tense interactions are harder than low education/low health literacy challenges.
AI.conclusion: MedImageEdu provides a controlled testbed to assess whether multimodal agents can genuinely teach from evidence rather than merely answer from text, addressing key gaps in current medical AI systems for patient education.

title: Material-Agnostic Zero-Shot Thermal Inference for Metal Additive Manufacturing via a Parametric PINN Framework
summary: Accurate thermal modeling in metal additive manufacturing (AM) is essential for understanding the process-structure-performance relationship. While prior studies have explored generalization across unseen process conditions, they often require extensive datasets, costly retraining, or pre-training. Generalization across different materials also remains relatively unexplored due to the challenges posed by distinct material-dependent thermal behaviors. This paper introduces a parametric physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework for zero-shot generalization across arbitrary materials without labeled data, retraining, or pre-training. The framework adopts a decoupled parametric PINN architecture that separately encodes material properties and spatiotemporal coordinates, fusing them through conditional modulation to better align with the multiplicative role of material parameters in the governing equation and boundary conditions. Physics-guided output scaling derived from Rosenthal's analytical solution and a hybrid optimization strategy are further incorporated to enhance physical consistency, training stability, and convergence. Experiments on bare plate laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) across diverse metal alloys, including both in-distribution and out-of-distribution cases, demonstrate effective zero-shot generalizability along with superior training efficiency. Specifically, the proposed framework achieved up to a 64.2% reduction in relative L2 error compared to the non-parametric baseline while surpassing its performance within only 4.4% of the baseline training epochs. Ablation studies confirm that the proposed framework's components are broadly applicable to other PINN-based approaches. Overall, the proposed framework provides an efficient and scalable material-agnostic solution for zero-shot thermal modeling, contributing to more flexible and practical deployment in metal AM.

AI.tldr: A parametric physics-informed neural network framework enables zero-shot generalization across different metal alloys for thermal modeling in additive manufacturing without retraining or labeled data.
AI.motivation: Current thermal modeling approaches in metal additive manufacturing require extensive datasets, costly retraining, or pre-training, and struggle with generalization across different materials due to their distinct thermal behaviors.
AI.method: A parametric PINN framework with decoupled architecture that separately encodes material properties and spatiotemporal coordinates, fused through conditional modulation. Includes physics-guided output scaling from Rosenthal's solution and hybrid optimization for enhanced physical consistency and training stability.
AI.result: The framework achieved effective zero-shot generalizability across diverse metal alloys in LPBF, with up to 64.2% reduction in relative L2 error compared to non-parametric baseline, while reaching superior performance in only 4.4% of baseline training epochs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides an efficient, scalable, material-agnostic solution for zero-shot thermal modeling, enabling more flexible and practical deployment in metal additive manufacturing applications.

title: Acceptance Dynamics Across Cognitive Domains in Speculative Decoding
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates large language model (LLM) inference. It uses a small draft model to propose a tree of future tokens. A larger target model then verifies these tokens in a single batched forward pass. Despite the growing body of work on speculative methods, the degree to which the cognitive characteristics of a task affect acceptance probability remains largely unexplored. We present an empirical study of tree-based speculative decoding acceptance dynamics. Our study spans four well-established NLP benchmark domains: code generation, mathematical reasoning, logical reasoning, and open-ended chat. For this, we use TinyLlama-1.1B as the draft model against Llama-2-7B-Chat-GPTQ as the target. Over 99,768 speculative nodes collected from 200 prompts, we derive per-domain acceptance rates, expected accepted lengths, depth-acceptance profiles, and entropy-acceptance correlations. We find that task type is a stronger predictor of acceptance than tree depth. Furthermore, only the chat domain consistently yields an expected accepted length exceeding 1.0 token per step. We also show that the entropy-acceptance correlation is consistently negative but weak across all domains (rho in [-0.20, -0.15]). Counterintuitively, chat produces the highest entropy yet the highest acceptance rate. We attribute this divergence to the lexical predictability of RLHF-aligned register. These findings have direct implications for domain-aware speculation budgets and draft-model selection strategies. Index Terms--speculative decoding, large language model inference, tree attention, draft model, acceptance probability, LLM efficiency

AI.tldr: An empirical study showing that task type is a stronger predictor of acceptance probability in tree-based speculative decoding than tree depth, with chat domains yielding the highest acceptance rates despite higher entropy.
AI.motivation: Despite growing work on speculative decoding methods, there's limited exploration of how cognitive characteristics of different tasks affect acceptance probability in tree-based speculative decoding.
AI.method: Empirical study using TinyLlama-1.1B as draft model and Llama-2-7B-Chat-GPTQ as target model across four NLP domains: code generation, mathematical reasoning, logical reasoning, and open-ended chat. Analyzed 99,768 speculative nodes from 200 prompts, measuring acceptance rates, expected accepted lengths, depth-acceptance profiles, and entropy-acceptance correlations.
AI.result: Task type is stronger predictor of acceptance than tree depth. Only chat domain consistently yields expected accepted length exceeding 1.0 token per step. Entropy-acceptance correlation is consistently negative but weak (rho in [-0.20, -0.15]). Counterintuitively, chat produces highest entropy yet highest acceptance rate, attributed to lexical predictability of RLHF-aligned register.
AI.conclusion: Findings have direct implications for domain-aware speculation budgets and draft-model selection strategies in speculative decoding for LLM inference acceleration.

title: Physics-Informed Machine Learning for Pouch Cell Temperature Estimation
summary: Accurate temperature estimation of pouch cells with indirect liquid cooling is essential for optimizing battery thermal management systems for transportation electrification. However, it is challenging due to the computational expense of finite element simulations and the limitations of data-driven models. This paper presents a physics-informed machine learning (PIML) framework for the efficient and reliable estimation of steady-state temperature profiles. The PIML approach integrates the governing heat transfer equations directly into the neural network's loss function, enabling high-fidelity predictions with significantly faster convergence than purely data-driven methods. The framework is evaluated on a dataset of varying cooling channel geometries. Results demonstrate that the PIML model converges more rapidly and achieves markedly higher accuracy, with a 49.1% reduction in mean squared error over the data-driven model. Validation against independent test cases further confirms its superior performance, particularly in regions away from the cooling channels. These findings underscore the potential of PIML for surrogate modeling and design optimization in battery systems.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed machine learning framework accurately estimates temperature profiles in pouch cells with liquid cooling, achieving 49.1% lower error than data-driven models.
AI.motivation: Accurate temperature estimation of pouch cells with indirect liquid cooling is essential for battery thermal management systems in transportation electrification, but existing methods (finite element simulations and data-driven models) have computational expense and accuracy limitations.
AI.method: Developed a physics-informed machine learning (PIML) framework that integrates the governing heat transfer equations directly into the neural network's loss function, enabling efficient and reliable steady-state temperature profile estimation.
AI.result: The PIML model converges more rapidly and achieves significantly higher accuracy than purely data-driven methods, with a 49.1% reduction in mean squared error. Validation against independent test cases confirms superior performance, especially in regions away from cooling channels.
AI.conclusion: The PIML framework demonstrates strong potential for surrogate modeling and design optimization in battery thermal management systems, offering high-fidelity predictions with faster convergence than traditional approaches.

title: DR$^{3}$-Eval: Towards Realistic and Reproducible Deep Research Evaluation
summary: Deep Research Agents (DRAs) aim to solve complex, long-horizon research tasks involving planning, retrieval, multimodal understanding, and report generation, yet their evaluation remains challenging due to dynamic web environments and ambiguous task definitions. We propose DR$^{3}$-Eval, a realistic and reproducible benchmark for evaluating deep research agents on multimodal, multi-file report generation. DR$^{3}$-Eval is constructed from authentic user-provided materials and paired with a per-task static research sandbox corpus that simulates open-web complexity while remaining fully verifiable, containing supportive documents, distractors, and noise. Moreover, we introduce a multi-dimensional evaluation framework measuring Information Recall, Factual Accuracy, Citation Coverage, Instruction Following, and Depth Quality, and validate its alignment with human judgments. Experiments with our developed multi-agent system DR$^{3}$-Agent based on multiple state-of-the-art language models demonstrate that DR$^{3}$-Eval is highly challenging and reveals critical failure modes in retrieval robustness and hallucination control. Our code and data are publicly available.

AI.tldr: DR³-Eval is a benchmark for evaluating deep research agents on multimodal, multi-file report generation, featuring realistic materials and verifiable sandbox environments with multi-dimensional evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation of Deep Research Agents (DRAs) is challenging due to dynamic web environments and ambiguous task definitions, creating a need for realistic, reproducible benchmarks.
AI.method: Constructed DR³-Eval from authentic user-provided materials with per-task static research sandbox containing supportive documents, distractors, and noise. Introduced multi-dimensional evaluation framework measuring Information Recall, Factual Accuracy, Citation Coverage, Instruction Following, and Depth Quality.
AI.result: Experiments with DR³-Agent (multi-agent system based on state-of-the-art language models) show DR³-Eval is highly challenging, revealing critical failure modes in retrieval robustness and hallucination control. Evaluation framework aligns well with human judgments.
AI.conclusion: DR³-Eval provides a realistic and reproducible benchmark for evaluating deep research agents, addressing current evaluation challenges and offering valuable insights into agent capabilities and limitations through its multi-dimensional assessment framework.

title: Generative Augmented Inference
summary: Data-driven operations management often relies on parameters estimated from costly human-generated labels. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) and other AI systems offer inexpensive auxiliary data, but introduce a new challenge: AI outputs are not direct observations of the target outcomes, but could involve high-dimensional representations with complex and unknown relationships to human labels. Conventional methods leverage AI predictions as direct proxies for true labels, which can be inefficient or unreliable when this relationship is weak or misspecified. We propose Generative Augmented Inference (GAI), a general framework that incorporates AI-generated outputs as informative features for estimating models of human-labeled outcomes. GAI uses an orthogonal moment construction that enables consistent estimation and valid inference with flexible, nonparametric relationship between LLM-generated outputs and human labels. We establish asymptotic normality and show a "safe default" property: relative to human-data-only estimators, GAI weakly improves estimation efficiency under arbitrary auxiliary signals and yields strict gains whenever the auxiliary information is predictive. Empirically, GAI outperforms benchmarks across diverse settings. In conjoint analysis with weak auxiliary signals, GAI reduces estimation error by about 50% and lowers human labeling requirements by over 75%. In retail pricing, where all methods access the same auxiliary inputs, GAI consistently outperforms alternative estimators, highlighting the value of its construction rather than differences in information. In health insurance choice, it cuts labeling requirements by over 90% while maintaining decision accuracy. Across applications, GAI improves confidence interval coverage without inflating width. Overall, GAI provides a principled and scalable approach to integrating AI-generated information.

AI.tldr: Generative Augmented Inference (GAI) is a framework that uses AI-generated outputs as informative features (rather than direct proxies) for estimating models with human-labeled outcomes, improving efficiency and reducing labeling requirements while ensuring valid inference.
AI.motivation: Data-driven operations management relies on costly human-generated labels. While AI systems offer inexpensive auxiliary data, their outputs aren't direct observations of target outcomes and have complex relationships with human labels. Conventional methods that use AI predictions as direct proxies can be inefficient or unreliable when the relationship is weak or misspecified.
AI.method: GAI incorporates AI-generated outputs as informative features for estimating models of human-labeled outcomes. It uses an orthogonal moment construction that enables consistent estimation and valid inference with flexible, nonparametric relationships between AI outputs and human labels. This approach doesn't require strong assumptions about the AI-human label relationship.
AI.result: Theoretical: GAI ensures consistent estimation, establishes asymptotic normality, and has a "safe default" property - it weakly improves efficiency over human-data-only estimators under arbitrary auxiliary signals, with strict gains when auxiliary information is predictive. Empirical: GAI outperforms benchmarks across applications: reduces estimation error by ~50% and labeling needs by >75% in conjoint analysis; consistently beats alternatives in retail pricing; cuts labeling by >90% in health insurance while maintaining accuracy; improves confidence interval coverage without inflating width.
AI.conclusion: GAI provides a principled and scalable approach to integrating AI-generated information into operations management, overcoming limitations of using AI outputs as direct proxies. It offers reliable efficiency gains while maintaining statistical validity, making it a valuable tool for leveraging inexpensive AI data alongside costly human labels.

title: M2-PALE: A Framework for Explaining Multi-Agent MCTS--Minimax Hybrids via Process Mining and LLMs
summary: Monte-Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) is a fundamental sampling-based search algorithm widely used for online planning in sequential decision-making domains. Despite its success in driving recent advances in artificial intelligence, understanding the behavior of MCTS agents remains a challenge for both developers and users. This difficulty stems from the complex search trees produced through the simulation of numerous future states and their intricate relationships. A known weakness of standard MCTS is its reliance on highly selective tree construction, which may lead to the omission of crucial moves and a vulnerability to tactical traps. To resolve this, we incorporate shallow, full-width Minimax search into the rollout phase of multi-agent MCTS to enhance strategic depth. Furthermore, to demystify the resulting decision-making logic, we introduce \textsf{M2-PALE} (MCTS--Minimax Process-Aided Linguistic Explanations). This framework employs process mining techniques, specifically the Alpha Miner, iDHM, and Inductive Miner algorithms, to extract underlying behavioral workflows from agent execution traces. These process models are then synthesized by LLMs to generate human-readable causal and distal explanations. We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach in a small-scale checkers environment, establishing a scalable foundation for interpreting hybrid agents in increasingly complex strategic domains.

AI.tldr: MCTS-Minimax hybrid agent with process mining and LLM-based explanations for strategic decision-making in checkers.
AI.motivation: MCTS is widely used but difficult to interpret, and has weaknesses in selective tree construction that may miss crucial moves and be vulnerable to tactical traps. There's a need for both better strategic depth and better explanation of decision-making logic.
AI.method: Incorporates shallow, full-width Minimax search into MCTS rollout phase for enhanced strategic depth. Uses process mining (Alpha Miner, iDHM, Inductive Miner) to extract behavioral workflows from agent execution traces, then synthesizes them with LLMs to generate human-readable causal and distal explanations.
AI.result: Developed M2-PALE framework for explaining MCTS-Minimax hybrid agents. Demonstrated efficacy in a small-scale checkers environment, establishing a scalable foundation for interpretability in complex strategic domains.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach addresses both MCTS weaknesses (through Minimax integration) and interpretability challenges (through process mining and LLM synthesis), providing a foundation for more explainable and strategically sound agents in complex domains.

title: From Risk to Rescue: An Agentic Survival Analysis Framework for Liquidation Prevention
summary: Decentralized Finance (DeFi) lending protocols like Aave v3 rely on over-collateralization to secure loans, yet users frequently face liquidation due to volatile market conditions. Existing risk management tools utilize static health-factor thresholds, which are reactive and fail to distinguish between administrative "dust" cleanup and genuine insolvency. In this work, we propose an autonomous agent that leverages time-to-event (survival) analysis and moves beyond prediction to execution. Unlike passive risk signals, this agent perceives risk, simulates counterfactual futures, and executes protocol-faithful interventions to proactively prevent liquidations. We introduce a return period metric derived from a numerically stable XGBoost Cox proportional hazards model to normalize risk across transaction types, coupled with a volatility-adjusted trend score to filter transient market noise. To select optimal interventions, we implement a counterfactual optimization loop that simulates potential user actions to find the minimum capital required to mitigate risk. We validate our approach using a high-fidelity, protocol-faithful Aave v3 simulator on a cohort of 4,882 high-risk user profiles. The results demonstrate the agent's ability to prevent liquidations in imminent-risk scenarios where static rules fail, effectively "saving the unsavable" while maintaining a zero worsening rate, providing a critical safety guarantee often missing in autonomous financial agents. Furthermore, the system successfully differentiates between actionable financial risks and negligible dust events, optimizing capital efficiency where static rules fail.

AI.tldr: An autonomous agent uses survival analysis and counterfactual optimization to proactively prevent liquidations in DeFi lending protocols, moving beyond reactive static thresholds to save high-risk positions while maintaining zero worsening rate.
AI.motivation: Current DeFi lending protocols like Aave v3 rely on static health-factor thresholds that are reactive and fail to distinguish between administrative dust cleanup and genuine insolvency, leading to unnecessary liquidations during market volatility.
AI.method: Proposes an autonomous agent using time-to-event (survival) analysis with an XGBoost Cox proportional hazards model for risk normalization, volatility-adjusted trend scoring to filter market noise, and counterfactual optimization loops to simulate potential user actions for finding minimum capital interventions.
AI.result: Validated on 4,882 high-risk user profiles using a high-fidelity Aave v3 simulator, the agent successfully prevents liquidations in imminent-risk scenarios where static rules fail, maintains zero worsening rate, differentiates actionable risks from dust events, and optimizes capital efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The autonomous agent provides a proactive, protocol-faithful risk management solution that can prevent liquidations while maintaining critical safety guarantees, offering superior performance compared to static threshold approaches in DeFi lending protocols.

title: CAMO: An Agentic Framework for Automated Causal Discovery from Micro Behaviors to Macro Emergence in LLM Agent Simulations
summary: LLM-empowered agent simulations are increasingly used to study social emergence, yet the micro-to-macro causal mechanisms behind macro outcomes often remain unclear. This is challenging because emergence arises from intertwined agent interactions and meso-level feedback and nonlinearity, making generative mechanisms hard to disentangle. To this end, we introduce \textbf{\textsc{CAMO}}, an automated \textbf{Ca}usal discovery framework from \textbf{M}icr\textbf{o} behaviors to \textbf{M}acr\textbf{o} Emergence in LLM agent simulations. \textsc{CAMO} converts mechanistic hypotheses into computable factors grounded in simulation records and learns a compact causal representation centered on an emergent target $Y$. \textsc{CAMO} outputs a computable Markov boundary and a minimal upstream explanatory subgraph, yielding interpretable causal chains and actionable intervention levers. It also uses simulator-internal counterfactual probing to orient ambiguous edges and revise hypotheses when evidence contradicts the current view. Experiments across four emergent settings demonstrate the promise of \textsc{CAMO}.

AI.tldr: CAMO: An automated causal discovery framework that identifies micro-to-macro causal mechanisms in LLM-agent simulations, explaining emergent social phenomena through computational causal analysis.
AI.motivation: While LLM-empowered agent simulations are increasingly used to study social emergence, the micro-to-macro causal mechanisms behind macro outcomes often remain unclear. The challenge is that emergence arises from complex, intertwined agent interactions and meso-level feedback loops with nonlinearity, making it difficult to disentangle the underlying generative mechanisms.
AI.method: CAMO converts mechanistic hypotheses into computable factors grounded in simulation records and learns a compact causal representation centered on an emergent target Y. It outputs a computable Markov boundary and a minimal upstream explanatory subgraph to yield interpretable causal chains. The framework also uses simulator-internal counterfactual probing to orient ambiguous edges and revise hypotheses when evidence contradicts the current view.
AI.result: Experiments across four emergent settings demonstrate the promise of CAMO, showing it can effectively identify causal mechanisms from micro behaviors to macro emergence in LLM agent simulations.
AI.conclusion: CAMO provides an automated approach to uncover and explain the causal pathways from individual agent behaviors to emergent social phenomena, offering interpretable causal chains and actionable intervention points for complex simulation studies.

title: CLion: Efficient Cautious Lion Optimizer with Enhanced Generalization
summary:
Lion optimizer is a popular learning-based optimization algorithm in machine learning, which shows impressive performance in training many deep learning models. Although convergence property of the Lion optimizer has been studied, its generalization analysis is still missing. To fill this gap, we study generalization property of the Lion via algorithmic stability based on the mathematical induction. Specifically, we prove that the Lion has a generalization error of $O(\frac{1}{Nτ^T})$, where $N$ is training sample size, and $τ>0$ denotes the smallest absolute value of non-zero element in gradient estimator, and $T$ is the total iteration number. In addition, we obtain an interesting byproduct that the SignSGD algorithm has the same generalization error as the Lion. To enhance generalization of the Lion, we design a novel efficient Cautious Lion (i.e., CLion) optimizer by cautiously using sign function. Moreover, we prove that our CLion has a lower generalization error of $O(\frac{1}{N})$ than $O(\frac{1}{Nτ^T})$ of the Lion, since the parameter $τ$ generally is very small. Meanwhile, we study convergence property of our CLion optimizer, and prove that our CLion has a fast convergence rate of $O(\frac{\sqrt{d}}{T^{1/4}})$ under $\ell_1$-norm of gradient for nonconvex stochastic optimization, where $d$ denotes the model dimension.
  Extensive numerical experiments demonstrate effectiveness of our CLion optimizer.

AI.tldr: The paper provides generalization analysis for the Lion optimizer, shows SignSGD shares the same generalization error, introduces an improved CLion optimizer with better generalization guarantees, and proves CLion's convergence properties.
AI.motivation: Although the Lion optimizer has been studied for convergence properties, its generalization analysis is still missing. The authors aim to fill this gap by analyzing generalization properties and designing an improved optimizer with better generalization guarantees.
AI.method: Used algorithmic stability based on mathematical induction to analyze generalization of Lion optimizer. Designed a novel CLion optimizer by cautiously using sign function to enhance generalization. Analyzed convergence properties using theoretical analysis of nonconvex stochastic optimization.
AI.result: Proved Lion has generalization error of O(1/(Nτ^T)) where τ is smallest absolute value of non-zero gradient element. Showed SignSGD shares same generalization error. CLion achieves better generalization error of O(1/N) and has convergence rate of O(√d/T^{1/4}) under ℓ₁-norm of gradient. Experimental results confirm effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully fills the generalization analysis gap for Lion optimizer, reveals interesting connection with SignSGD, introduces CLion with superior generalization guarantees and proven convergence properties, providing both theoretical insights and practical improvements for sign-based optimization methods.
