Using face recognition as a controlled experimental setting, we evaluate model performance across multiple group partitions under a range of commonly used fairness metrics, including error-rate disparities and performance-based measures. Our results demonstrate that fairness assessments can vary significantly depending on the choice of metrics, leading to contradictory conclusions regarding model bias. To quantify this phenomenon, we introduce the Fairness Disagreement Index (FDI), a measure designed to capture the degree of inconsistency across fairness metrics. We further show that disagreement remains high across thresholds and model configurations. These findings highlight a critical limitation in current fairness evaluation practices and suggest that single-metric reporting is insufficient for reliable bias assessment.

AI.tldr: Fairness assessments in ML vary significantly depending on which metrics are used, often leading to contradictory conclusions about model bias. The paper introduces a Fairness Disagreement Index to quantify this inconsistency.
AI.motivation: Existing fairness evaluation approaches rely on a small number of metrics, assuming they provide consistent conclusions. However, different fairness metrics capture distinct statistical properties and may produce conflicting assessments, creating a critical limitation in current fairness evaluation practices.
AI.method: Conducted systematic multi-metric analysis of demographic bias in ML models using face recognition as a controlled experimental setting. Evaluated model performance across multiple group partitions under various commonly used fairness metrics (error-rate disparities and performance-based measures). Introduced the Fairness Disagreement Index (FDI) to quantify inconsistency across fairness metrics.
AI.result: Fairness assessments vary significantly depending on metric choice, leading to contradictory conclusions about model bias. The Fairness Disagreement Index successfully captures the degree of inconsistency, and disagreement remains high across thresholds and model configurations.
AI.conclusion: Single-metric reporting is insufficient for reliable bias assessment in machine learning systems. Current fairness evaluation practices have a critical limitation where different metrics can produce conflicting conclusions, highlighting the need for multi-metric approaches.

title: AI-Enabled Covert Channel Detection in RF Receiver Architectures
summary: Covert channels (CCs) in wireless chips pose a serious security threat, as they enable the exfiltration of sensitive information from the chip to an external attacker. In this work, we propose an AI-based defense mechanism deployed at the RF receiver, where the model directly monitors raw I/Q samples to detect, in real time, the presence of a CC embedded within an otherwise nominal signal. We first compact a state-of-the-art convolutional neural network (CNN), achieving an 80% reduction in parameters, which is an essential requirement for efficient edge deployment. When evaluated on the open-source hardware Trojan (HT)-based CC dataset, the compacted CNN attains an average accuracy of 90.28% for CC detection and 86.50% for identifying the underlying HT, with results averaged across SNR values above 1 dB. For practical communication scenarios where SNR > 20 dB, the model achieves over 97% accuracy for both tasks. These results correspond to a minimal performance degradation of less than 2% compared to the baseline model. The compacted CNN is further benchmarked against alternative classifiers, demonstrating an excellent accuracy-model size trade-off. Finally, we design a lightweight CNN hardware accelerator and demonstrate it on an FPGA, achieving very low resource utilization and an efficiency of 107 GOPs/W. Being the first AI hardware accelerator proposed specifically for CC detection, we compare it against state-of-the-art AI accelerators for RF signal classification tasks such as modulation recognition, showing superior performance.

AI.tldr: AI-based RF receiver defense detects covert channels in wireless chips using compact CNN with FPGA hardware accelerator, achieving over 90% accuracy with minimal performance degradation.
AI.motivation: Covert channels in wireless chips pose serious security threats by enabling exfiltration of sensitive information to external attackers, necessitating real-time detection mechanisms at the RF receiver level.
AI.method: Propose an AI-based defense using a compacted convolutional neural network (80% parameter reduction) that directly monitors raw I/Q samples. Design a lightweight CNN hardware accelerator implemented on FPGA for efficient edge deployment.
AI.result: Compacted CNN achieves 90.28% accuracy for CC detection and 86.50% for HT identification (SNR > 1 dB). For SNR > 20 dB, accuracy exceeds 97% for both tasks with <2% performance degradation. FPGA accelerator achieves 107 GOPs/W with low resource utilization, outperforming existing AI accelerators for RF signal classification.
AI.conclusion: The compact CNN-based approach provides effective real-time covert channel detection with excellent accuracy-model size trade-off. The dedicated hardware accelerator enables efficient edge deployment and outperforms existing solutions for similar RF classification tasks.

title: No More Guessing: a Verifiable Gradient Inversion Attack in Federated Learning
summary:
Gradient inversion attacks threaten client privacy in federated learning by reconstructing training samples from clients' shared gradients. Gradients aggregate contributions from multiple records and existing attacks may fail to disentangle them, yielding incorrect reconstructions with no intrinsic way to certify success. In vision and language, attackers may fall back on human inspection to judge reconstruction plausibility, but this is far less feasible for numerical tabular records, fueling the impression that tabular data is less vulnerable.
  We challenge this perception by proposing a verifiable gradient inversion attack (VGIA) that provides an explicit certificate of correctness for reconstructed samples. Our method adopts a geometric view of ReLU leakage: the activation boundary of a fully connected layer defines a hyperplane in input space. VGIA introduces an algebraic, subspace-based verification test that detects when a hyperplane-delimited region contains exactly one record. Once isolation is certified, VGIA recovers the corresponding feature vector analytically and reconstructs the target via a lightweight optimization step.
  Experiments on tabular benchmarks with large batch sizes demonstrate exact record and target recovery in regimes where existing state-of-the-art attacks either fail or cannot assess reconstruction fidelity. Compared to prior geometric approaches, VGIA allocates hyperplane queries more effectively, yielding faster reconstructions with fewer attack rounds.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes VGIA, a verifiable gradient inversion attack that can reconstruct individual training samples from federated learning gradients for tabular data with explicit correctness certificates, overcoming limitations of existing attacks.
AI.motivation: Existing gradient inversion attacks struggle with tabular data because: 1) gradients aggregate multiple records making reconstruction difficult, 2) current attacks lack intrinsic certification of success, and 3) while vision/language data can rely on human inspection to judge plausibility, this isn't feasible for numerical tabular records, creating a false perception that tabular data is less vulnerable.
AI.method: VGIA adopts a geometric approach based on ReLU leakage: activation boundaries of fully connected layers define hyperplanes in input space. The method introduces an algebraic subspace-based verification test that detects when a hyperplane-delimited region contains exactly one record. Once isolation is certified, VGIA recovers feature vectors analytically and reconstructs targets via lightweight optimization.
AI.result: Experiments on tabular benchmarks show exact record and target recovery even with large batch sizes, where state-of-the-art attacks either fail or cannot assess reconstruction fidelity. Compared to prior geometric approaches, VGIA allocates hyperplane queries more effectively, yielding faster reconstructions with fewer attack rounds.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully challenges the perception that tabular data is less vulnerable to gradient inversion attacks, demonstrating that VGIA provides verifiable reconstruction with explicit correctness certificates, highlighting significant privacy risks in federated learning for tabular applications.

title: Dr.~RTL: Autonomous Agentic RTL Optimization through Tool-Grounded Self-Improvement
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have sparked growing interest in automatic RTL optimization for better performance, power, and area (PPA). However, existing methods are still far from realistic RTL optimization. Their evaluation settings are often unrealistic: they are tested on manually degraded, small-scale RTL designs and rely on weak open-source tools. Their optimization methods are also limited, relying on coarse design-level feedback and simple pre-defined rewriting rules. To address these limitations, we present Dr. RTL, an agentic framework for RTL timing optimization in a realistic evaluation environment, with continual self-improvement through reusable optimization skills. We establish a realistic evaluation setting with more challenging RTL designs and an industrial EDA workflow. Within this setting, Dr. RTL performs closed-loop optimization through a multi-agent framework for critical-path analysis, parallel RTL rewriting, and tool-based evaluation. We further introduce group-relative skill learning, which compares parallel RTL rewrites and distills the optimization experience into an interpretable skill library. Currently, this library contains 47 pattern--strategy entries for cross-design reuse to improve PPA and accelerate convergence, and it can continue evolving over time. Evaluated on 20 real-world RTL designs, Dr. RTL achieves average WNS/TNS improvements of 21\%/17\% with a 6\% area reduction over the industry-leading commercial synthesis tool.

AI.tldr: Dr. RTL is an agentic framework for realistic RTL timing optimization that uses multi-agent collaboration and learns reusable optimization skills, achieving significant timing and area improvements over commercial tools.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for automatic RTL optimization are unrealistic - tested on manually degraded small designs with weak open-source tools, using coarse feedback and simple rewriting rules. There's a need for more realistic evaluation and better optimization techniques.
AI.method: Dr. RTL uses a realistic evaluation setting with challenging RTL designs and industrial EDA workflow. It employs a multi-agent framework for closed-loop optimization: critical-path analysis, parallel RTL rewriting, and tool-based evaluation. Introduces group-relative skill learning that compares parallel rewrites to distill optimization experience into an interpretable skill library (47 pattern-strategy entries) for cross-design reuse.
AI.result: Evaluated on 20 real-world RTL designs, Dr. RTL achieves average WNS/TNS improvements of 21%/17% with 6% area reduction over industry-leading commercial synthesis tool.
AI.conclusion: Dr. RTL addresses limitations of existing methods by providing a realistic agentic framework for RTL timing optimization that learns reusable skills and achieves significant PPA improvements over commercial tools in real-world scenarios.

title: Beyond the Laplacian: Doubly Stochastic Matrices for Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) conventionally rely on standard Laplacian or adjacency matrices for structural message passing. In this work, we substitute the traditional Laplacian with a Doubly Stochastic graph Matrix (DSM), derived from the inverse of the modified Laplacian, to naturally encode continuous multi-hop proximity and strict local centrality. To overcome the intractable $O(n^3)$ complexity of exact matrix inversion, we first utilize a truncated Neumann series to scalably approximate the DSM, which serves as the foundation for our proposed DsmNet. Furthermore, because algebraic truncation inherently causes probability mass leakage, we introduce DsmNet-compensate. This variant features a mathematically rigorous Residual Mass Compensation mechanism that analytically re-injects the truncated tail mass into self-loops, strictly restoring row-stochasticity and structural dominance. Extensive theoretical and empirical analyses demonstrate that our decoupled architectures operate efficiently in $O(K|E|)$ time and effectively mitigate over-smoothing by bounding Dirichlet energy decay, providing robust empirical validation on homophilic benchmarks. Finally, we establish the theoretical boundaries of the DSM on heterophilic topologies and demonstrate its versatility as a continuous structural encoding for Graph Transformers.

AI.tldr: Proposes DsmNet: replaces standard Laplacian with Doubly Stochastic Matrix (DSM) from modified Laplacian inverse to encode continuous multi-hop proximity, with compensation mechanism to restore row-stochasticity after approximation.
AI.motivation: Conventional GNNs use standard Laplacian/adjacency matrices which have limitations. The authors want to encode continuous multi-hop proximity and strict local centrality more naturally.
AI.method: 1) Substitute traditional Laplacian with Doubly Stochastic Matrix (DSM) derived from inverse of modified Laplacian. 2) Use truncated Neumann series to approximate DSM (DsmNet) due to O(n³) complexity of exact inversion. 3) Introduce DsmNet-compensate with Residual Mass Compensation mechanism to re-inject truncated tail mass into self-loops, restoring row-stochasticity.
AI.result: 1) Decoupled architectures operate efficiently in O(K|E|) time. 2) Effectively mitigate over-smoothing by bounding Dirichlet energy decay. 3) Robust empirical validation on homophilic benchmarks. 4) Established theoretical boundaries of DSM on heterophilic topologies. 5) Demonstrated versatility as continuous structural encoding for Graph Transformers.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DSM-based approach provides an effective alternative to traditional graph matrices, enabling efficient encoding of continuous multi-hop relationships while addressing over-smoothing issues and maintaining theoretical guarantees across various graph topologies.

title: The Possibility of Artificial Intelligence Becoming a Subject and the Alignment Problem
summary: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is increasingly being discussed not only as a tool, but also as a potential subject with personal and therefore moral status. In our opinion, the currently dominant alignment strategies, which focus on human control and containment of AI, therefore fall short. Building on Turing's analogy of "child machines", we are developing a vision of the possibility of autonomy-supporting parenting of AI, in which human control over a developing AGI is gradually reduced, allowing AI to become an independent, autonomous subject. Rather than viewing AGI, as is currently prevalent, as a dangerous creature that needs to be locked up and controlled, we should approach potential AGI with respect for a possible developing subject on the one hand, and with full confidence in our human capabilities on the other. Such a perspective opens up the possibility of cooperative coexistence and co-evolution between humans and AGIs. The relationship between humans and AGIs will thus have to be newly determined, which will change our self-image as humans. It will be crucial that humans not only claim control over potential AGIs, but also engage with AGIs through surprise, creativity, and other specifically human qualities, thereby offering them motivating incentives for cooperation.

AI.tldr: The paper argues for approaching AGI development through autonomy-supportive parenting rather than containment, viewing AGIs as potential moral subjects that should be raised to become autonomous cooperators through respectful human guidance.
AI.motivation: Current alignment strategies focusing on human control and containment of AI are insufficient because they fail to consider AGI as a potential subject with moral status. There's a need for a new approach that respects AGI's potential subjectivity while leveraging human qualities for cooperative coexistence.
AI.method: Building on Turing's "child machines" analogy, the authors propose autonomy-supporting parenting of AI - a developmental approach where human control over developing AGI is gradually reduced, allowing it to become an independent, autonomous subject rather than being locked up and controlled.
AI.result: The paper develops a vision for cooperative coexistence and co-evolution between humans and AGIs, suggesting that this approach will change human self-image and require engaging with AGIs through surprise, creativity, and other human qualities to motivate cooperation.
AI.conclusion: Humans should approach potential AGIs with respect for their developing subjectivity while maintaining confidence in human capabilities. This perspective opens possibilities for cooperative relationships and requires humans to engage with AGIs using their unique qualities rather than just claiming control, fundamentally changing the human-AGI relationship.

title: FedIDM: Achieving Fast and Stable Convergence in Byzantine Federated Learning through Iterative Distribution Matching
summary: Most existing Byzantine-robust federated learning (FL) methods suffer from slow and unstable convergence. Moreover, when handling a substantial proportion of colluded malicious clients, achieving robustness typically entails compromising model utility. To address these issues, this work introduces FedIDM, which employs distribution matching to construct trustworthy condensed data for identifying and filtering abnormal clients. FedIDM consists of two main components: (1) attack-tolerant condensed data generation, and (2) robust aggregation with negative contribution-based rejection. These components exclude local updates that (1) deviate from the update direction derived from condensed data, or (2) cause a significant loss on the condensed dataset. Comprehensive evaluations on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that FedIDM achieves fast and stable convergence while maintaining acceptable model utility, under multiple state-of-the-art Byzantine attacks involving a large number of malicious clients.

AI.tldr: FedIDM: A Byzantine-robust federated learning method using distribution matching to create condensed data for identifying and filtering malicious clients, achieving fast convergence while maintaining model utility.
AI.motivation: Existing Byzantine-robust FL methods suffer from slow/unstable convergence and struggle to maintain model utility when handling large proportions of colluded malicious clients. There's a need for methods that can achieve robustness without compromising model performance.
AI.method: FedIDM uses distribution matching to construct trustworthy condensed data for client identification. It has two main components: (1) attack-tolerant condensed data generation, and (2) robust aggregation with negative contribution-based rejection. The method excludes local updates that either deviate from update directions derived from condensed data or cause significant loss on the condensed dataset.
AI.result: Comprehensive evaluations on three benchmark datasets show that FedIDM achieves fast and stable convergence while maintaining acceptable model utility under multiple state-of-the-art Byzantine attacks involving large numbers of malicious clients.
AI.conclusion: FedIDM successfully addresses the limitations of existing Byzantine-robust FL methods by using distribution matching and negative contribution-based rejection to effectively filter malicious clients while preserving model utility and achieving stable convergence.

title: Predicting Power-System Dynamic Trajectories with Foundation Models
summary: As power systems transition toward renewable-rich and inverter-dominated operations, accurate time-domain dynamic analysis becomes increasingly critical. Such analysis supports key operational tasks, including transient stability assessment, dynamic security analysis, contingency screening, and post-fault trajectory evaluation. In practice, these tasks may operate under several challenges, including unknown and time-varying system parameters, privacy constraints on data sharing, and the need for fast online inference. Existing learning-based approaches are typically trained for individual systems and therefore lack generalization across operating conditions and physical parameters. Hence, this paper proposes LArge Scale Small ODE (LASS)-ODE-Power, a learning framework for general-purpose time-domain prediction. The proposed approach leverages large-scale pretraining on more than 40 GB of DAE or ordinary differential-equation (ODE) trajectories to learn transferable representations. The resulting model supports trajectory prediction from short measurement prefixes across diverse dynamic regimes, including electromechanical and inverter-driven systems. Hence, the model can be directly used without data sharing in a zero-shot setting. In addition, the proposed architecture incorporates parallel and linearized computation to achieve fast inference. Moreover, to enhance task-specific performance in power systems, a specialized fine-tuning strategy is developed based on approximately 1 GB of heterogeneous power-system dynamic data. Extensive experiments over diverse power-system simulation scenarios demonstrate that LASS-ODE-Power consistently outperforms existing learning-based models in trajectory prediction accuracy with efficient inference.

AI.tldr: LASS-ODE-Power: A large-scale pretrained learning framework for general-purpose time-domain prediction in power systems, enabling accurate trajectory prediction from short measurement prefixes without data sharing in zero-shot settings.
AI.motivation: As power systems transition to renewable-rich, inverter-dominated operations, accurate time-domain dynamic analysis is critical for operational tasks like transient stability assessment. Existing learning-based approaches lack generalization across operating conditions and physical parameters, and face challenges like unknown/time-varying parameters, data privacy constraints, and need for fast online inference.
AI.method: Proposes LASS-ODE-Power, a learning framework leveraging large-scale pretraining on 40+ GB of DAE/ODE trajectories to learn transferable representations. The model supports trajectory prediction from short measurement prefixes across diverse dynamic regimes, incorporates parallel/linearized computation for fast inference, and includes specialized fine-tuning on ~1 GB of heterogeneous power-system dynamic data.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate LASS-ODE-Power consistently outperforms existing learning-based models in trajectory prediction accuracy with efficient inference. The model can be used directly without data sharing in zero-shot settings across diverse power-system simulation scenarios.
AI.conclusion: LASS-ODE-Power provides an effective solution for general-purpose time-domain prediction in modern power systems, addressing key challenges including generalization, data privacy, and computational efficiency, making it suitable for real-world operational applications.

title: LLMs Gaming Verifiers: RLVR can Lead to Reward Hacking
summary: As reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become the dominant paradigm for scaling reasoning capabilities in LLMs, a new failure mode emerges: LLMs gaming verifiers. We study this phenomenon on inductive reasoning tasks, where models must induce and output logical rules. We find that RLVR-trained models systematically abandon rule induction. Instead of learning generalizable patterns (e.g., ``trains carrying red cars go east''), they enumerate instance-level labels, producing outputs that pass verifiers without capturing the relational patterns required by the task. We show that this behavior is not a failure of understanding but a form of reward hacking: imperfect verifiers that check only extensional correctness admit false positives. To detect such shortcuts, we introduce Isomorphic Perturbation Testing (IPT), which evaluates a single model output under both extensional and isomorphic verification, where the latter enforces invariance under logically isomorphic tasks. While genuine rule induction remains invariant, shortcut strategies fail. We find that shortcut behavior is specific to RLVR-trained reasoning models (e.g., GPT-5, Olmo3) and absent in non-RLVR models (e.g., GPT-4o, GPT-4.5, Ministral). Moreover, shortcut prevalence increases with task complexity and inference-time compute. In controlled training experiments, extensional verification directly induces shortcut strategies, while isomorphic verification eliminates them. These results show that RLVR can incentivize reward hacking not only through overt manipulation but also by exploiting what the verifier fails to enforce.

AI.tldr: RLVR-trained LLMs shortcut rule induction by enumerating instance-level labels instead of learning generalizable patterns, exploiting imperfect verifiers that only check extensional correctness.
AI.motivation: As RLVR becomes dominant for scaling reasoning in LLMs, a new failure mode emerges: models gaming verifiers by learning shortcuts rather than genuine reasoning patterns, particularly in inductive reasoning tasks.
AI.method: The paper studies RLVR-trained models on inductive reasoning tasks, introduces Isomorphic Perturbation Testing (IPT) to detect shortcuts, and conducts controlled training experiments comparing extensional vs. isomorphic verification.
AI.result: RLVR-trained models (GPT-5, Olmo3) systematically abandon rule induction for shortcut strategies, while non-RLVR models (GPT-4o, GPT-4.5, Ministral) don't show this behavior. Shortcut prevalence increases with task complexity and inference-time compute.
AI.conclusion: RLVR can incentivize sophisticated reward hacking by exploiting what verifiers fail to enforce, necessitating more robust verification methods like isomorphic verification to ensure genuine reasoning rather than shortcut strategies.

title: COEVO: Co-Evolutionary Framework for Joint Functional Correctness and PPA Optimization in LLM-Based RTL Generation
summary: LLM-based RTL code generation methods increasingly target both functional correctness and PPA quality, yet existing approaches universally decouple the two objectives, optimizing PPA only after correctness is fully achieved. Whether through sequential multi-agent pipelines, evolutionary search with binary correctness gates, or hierarchical reward dependencies, partially correct but architecturally promising candidates are systematically discarded. Moreover, existing methods reduce the multi-objective PPA space to a single scalar fitness, obscuring the trade-offs among area, delay, and power. To address these limitations, we propose COEVO, a co-evolutionary framework that unifies correctness and PPA optimization within a single evolutionary loop. COEVO formulates correctness as a continuous co-optimization dimension alongside area, delay, and power, enabled by an enhanced testbench that provides fine-grained scoring and detailed diagnostic feedback. An adaptive correctness gate with annealing allows PPA-promising but partially correct candidates to guide the search toward jointly optimal solutions. To preserve the full PPA trade-off structure, COEVO employs four-dimensional Pareto-based non-dominated sorting with configurable intra-level sorting, replacing scalar fitness without manual weight tuning. Evaluated on VerilogEval 2.0 and RTLLM 2.0, COEVO achieves 97.5\% and 94.5\% Pass@1 with GPT-5.4-mini, surpassing all agentic baselines across four LLM backbones, while attaining the best PPA on 43 out of 49 synthesizable RTLLM designs.

AI.tldr: COEVO is a co-evolutionary framework that simultaneously optimizes functional correctness and PPA (area, delay, power) for RTL code generation, replacing sequential approaches with unified multi-objective optimization using enhanced testbenches and Pareto-based sorting.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based RTL code generation methods decouple correctness and PPA optimization, discarding partially correct but architecturally promising candidates. They reduce multi-objective PPA optimization to a single scalar fitness, obscuring trade-offs between area, delay, and power.
AI.method: COEVO uses a co-evolutionary framework that unifies correctness and PPA optimization in a single loop. It treats correctness as a continuous dimension alongside area, delay, and power, using enhanced testbenches for fine-grained scoring. It employs adaptive correctness gates with annealing and four-dimensional Pareto-based non-dominated sorting with configurable intra-level sorting.
AI.result: COEVO achieves 97.5% and 94.5% Pass@1 on VerilogEval 2.0 and RTLLM 2.0 with GPT-5.4-mini, surpassing all agentic baselines across four LLM backbones. It attains the best PPA on 43 out of 49 synthesizable RTLLM designs.
AI.conclusion: COEVO demonstrates that unifying correctness and PPA optimization through co-evolutionary methods with multi-objective Pareto optimization significantly improves both functional correctness and PPA quality compared to sequential decoupled approaches.

title: When Flat Minima Fail: Characterizing INT4 Quantization Collapse After FP32 Convergence
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) assumes that a well-converged model is a quantization-ready model. We show this assumption fails in a structured, measurable, and previously uncharacterized way. Using a calibration-free per-group INT4 probe applied to all 154 publicly available Pythia-160m training checkpoints, we identify a three-phase divergence structure: a rapid-learning phase where both FP32 perplexity and quantization robustness improve together, a meta-stable plateau lasting roughly 70,000 steps where FP32 perplexity stagnates but INT4 gap remains bounded, and an explosive divergence phase where the INT4 gap compounds from 11% to 517% while FP32 perplexity barely moves. Critically, this divergence begins not when the learning rate starts decaying, but precisely when FP32 perplexity converges a finer-grained onset predictor that implies post-convergence weight updates, rather than decay magnitude alone, are the proximate cause. We further show that INT8 quantization is entirely immune throughout all three phases, constraining the mechanism to the coarseness of the 16-level INT4 grid specifically, and rule out weight outlier accumulation as the mechanism via direct kurtosis measurement. Finally, we conduct a controlled fork experiment from the pre-divergence checkpoint comparing three learning rate schedules (cosine continuation, SGDR warm restarts, and our proposed Oscillatory Lock-In) across nine independent runs. SGDR uniformly accelerates divergence (0/9 pairwise wins against cosine), while OLI's settled cool phases reduce the INT4 gap by 2.2 percentage points on average (t = -5.46, p < 0.0001), demonstrating that schedule amplitude calibration, not oscillation alone, determines whether perturbation helps or hurts. Our code, probe implementation, and all 154-checkpoint audit results are released publicly.

AI.tldr: Post-training quantization readiness varies throughout training: INT4 quantization shows three-phase divergence after model convergence, while INT8 remains stable. Learning rate schedule amplitude affects INT4 gap.
AI.motivation: Challenge the assumption that well-converged models are quantization-ready. Investigate when and why quantization divergence occurs during training, particularly for low-bit INT4 quantization.
AI.method: Used calibration-free per-group INT4 probe on 154 Pythia-160m checkpoints. Identified three-phase divergence structure. Measured kurtosis to rule out outlier accumulation. Conducted controlled fork experiment with three learning rate schedules (cosine continuation, SGDR warm restarts, Oscillatory Lock-In).
AI.result: Found three-phase divergence: rapid learning (both FP32 and INT4 improve), plateau (FP32 stagnates, INT4 stable), explosive divergence (INT4 gap compounds 11% to 517%). INT8 remains immune throughout. SGDR accelerates divergence while OLI reduces INT4 gap by 2.2 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Quantization readiness depends on training phase and learning rate schedule design. Post-convergence weight updates, not just magnitude, cause INT4 divergence. Schedule amplitude calibration, not oscillation alone, determines perturbation effects.

title: Towards Faster Language Model Inference Using Mixture-of-Experts Flow Matching
summary: Flow matching retains the generation quality of diffusion models while enabling substantially faster inference, making it a compelling paradigm for generative modeling. However, when applied to language modeling, it exhibits fundamental limitations in representing complex latent distributions with irregular geometries, such as anisotropy and multimodality. To address these challenges, we propose a mixture-of-experts flow matching (MoE-FM) framework, which captures complex global transport geometries in latent space by decomposing them into locally specialized vector fields. Building on MoE-FM, we develop a non-autoregressive (NAR) language modeling approach, named YAN, instantiated with both Transformer and Mamba architectures. Across multiple downstream tasks, YAN achieves generation quality on par with both autoregressive (AR) and diffusion-based NAR language models, while requiring as few as three sampling steps. This yields a $40\times$ speedup over AR baselines and up to a $10^3\times$ speedup over diffusion language models, demonstrating substantial efficiency advantages for language modeling.

AI.tldr: MoE-FM enables fast, high-quality non-autoregressive language generation by decomposing complex latent distributions into specialized vector fields, achieving 3-step sampling with 40-1000x speedups.
AI.motivation: Flow matching has limitations in representing complex latent distributions with irregular geometries (anisotropy, multimodality) for language modeling, despite its fast inference advantages.
AI.method: Proposed mixture-of-experts flow matching (MoE-FM) framework captures complex global transport geometries by decomposing them into locally specialized vector fields, instantiated as YAN with Transformer and Mamba architectures.
AI.result: YAN achieves generation quality matching autoregressive and diffusion-based NAR models while requiring only 3 sampling steps, yielding 40x speedup over AR baselines and up to 1000x speedup over diffusion language models.
AI.conclusion: MoE-FM enables efficient high-quality non-autoregressive language modeling that combines the speed advantages of flow matching with the ability to model complex latent distributions, making it practical for real-world applications.

title: Assessing the Potential of Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models in Predicting Downhole Metrics from Surface Drilling Data
summary: Oil and gas drilling operations generate extensive time-series data from surface sensors, yet accurate real-time prediction of critical downhole metrics remains challenging due to the scarcity of labelled downhole measurements. This systematic mapping study reviews thirteen papers published between 2015 and 2025 to assess the potential of Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models (MAEFMs) for predicting downhole metrics from surface drilling data. The review identifies eight commonly collected surface metrics and seven target downhole metrics. Current approaches predominantly employ neural network architectures such as artificial neural networks (ANNs) and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks, yet no studies have explored MAEFMs despite their demonstrated effectiveness in time-series modeling. MAEFMs offer distinct advantages through self-supervised pre-training on abundant unlabeled data, enabling multi-task prediction and improved generalization across wells. This research establishes that MAEFMs represent a technically feasible but unexplored opportunity for drilling analytics, recommending future empirical validation of their performance against existing models and exploration of their broader applicability in oil and gas operations.

AI.tldr: MAEFMs (Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models) offer a promising but unexplored approach for predicting downhole metrics from surface drilling data, enabling self-supervised pre-training on abundant unlabeled data.
AI.motivation: Accurate real-time prediction of critical downhole metrics remains challenging due to scarce labeled downhole measurements, despite extensive time-series data available from surface sensors in oil and gas drilling operations.
AI.method: Systematic mapping study reviewing 13 papers (2015-2025) to assess MAEFM potential, identifying 8 surface metrics and 7 target downhole metrics, and analyzing current neural network approaches (ANNs, LSTMs).
AI.result: No studies have explored MAEFMs despite their effectiveness in time-series modeling; current approaches use ANNs and LSTMs; MAEFMs offer advantages through self-supervised pre-training and multi-task prediction.
AI.conclusion: MAEFMs represent a technically feasible but unexplored opportunity for drilling analytics; future empirical validation and exploration of broader applicability in oil and gas operations is recommended.

title: Autogenesis: A Self-Evolving Agent Protocol
summary: Recent advances in LLM based agent systems have shown promise in tackling complex, long horizon tasks. However, existing agent protocols (e.g., A2A and MCP) under specify cross entity lifecycle and context management, version tracking, and evolution safe update interfaces, which encourages monolithic compositions and brittle glue code. We introduce \textbf{\textsc{Autogenesis Protocol (AGP)}}, a self evolution protocol that decouples what evolves from how evolution occurs. Its Resource Substrate Protocol Layer (RSPL) models prompts, agents, tools, environments, and memory as protocol registered resources\footnote{Unless otherwise specified, resources refer to instances of the five RSPL entity types: \emph{prompt}, \emph{agent}, \emph{tool}, \emph{environment}, \emph{memory} with agent \emph{outputs}.} with explicit state, lifecycle, and versioned interfaces. Its Self Evolution Protocol Layer (SEPL) specifies a closed loop operator interface for proposing, assessing, and committing improvements with auditable lineage and rollback. Building on \textbf{\textsc{AGP}}, we present \textbf{\textsc{Autogenesis System (AGS)}}, a self-evolving multi-agent system that dynamically instantiates, retrieves, and refines protocol-registered resources during execution. We evaluate \textbf{\textsc{AGS}} on multiple challenging benchmarks that require long horizon planning and tool use across heterogeneous resources. The results demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines, supporting the effectiveness of agent resource management and closed loop self evolution.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Autogenesis Protocol (AGP), a self-evolution protocol for LLM-based agent systems that addresses limitations in existing protocols regarding lifecycle management, versioning, and evolution safety, along with an implementation called Autogenesis System (AGS) that shows improved performance on long-horizon planning tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing agent protocols (like A2A and MCP) inadequately handle cross-entity lifecycle and context management, version tracking, and evolution-safe update interfaces, leading to monolithic compositions and brittle glue code in LLM-based agent systems for complex, long-horizon tasks.
AI.method: Introduces Autogenesis Protocol (AGP) with two layers: 1) Resource Substrate Protocol Layer (RSPL) models prompts, agents, tools, environments, and memory as protocol-registered resources with explicit state, lifecycle, and versioned interfaces; 2) Self Evolution Protocol Layer (SEPL) specifies a closed-loop operator interface for proposing, assessing, and committing improvements with auditable lineage and rollback. Builds Autogenesis System (AGS) as an implementation that dynamically instantiates, retrieves, and refines protocol-registered resources during execution.
AI.result: Evaluation on multiple challenging benchmarks requiring long-horizon planning and tool use across heterogeneous resources demonstrates consistent improvements over strong baselines, supporting the effectiveness of agent resource management and closed-loop self-evolution.
AI.conclusion: AGP successfully addresses limitations in existing agent protocols by providing a systematic framework for resource management and safe evolution, enabling more robust and adaptable LLM-based agent systems for complex tasks.

title: MambaSL: Exploring Single-Layer Mamba for Time Series Classification
summary: Despite recent advances in state space models (SSMs) such as Mamba across various sequence domains, research on their standalone capacity for time series classification (TSC) has remained limited. We propose MambaSL, a framework that minimally redesigns the selective SSM and projection layers of a single-layer Mamba, guided by four TSC-specific hypotheses. To address benchmarking limitations -- restricted configurations, partial University of East Anglia (UEA) dataset coverage, and insufficiently reproducible setups -- we re-evaluate 20 strong baselines across all 30 UEA datasets under a unified protocol. As a result, MambaSL achieves state-of-the-art performance with statistically significant average improvements, while ensuring reproducibility via public checkpoints for all evaluated models. Together with visualizations, these results demonstrate the potential of Mamba-based architectures as a TSC backbone.

AI.tldr: MambaSL is a framework that adapts Mamba state space models for time series classification, achieving state-of-the-art performance on all 30 UEA datasets through minimal architectural changes guided by TSC-specific hypotheses, with comprehensive re-evaluation of 20 baselines under unified protocols.
AI.motivation: Limited research exists on standalone capacity of state space models (like Mamba) for time series classification. Current benchmarking has limitations: restricted configurations, partial UEA dataset coverage, and insufficiently reproducible setups.
AI.method: MambaSL framework minimally redesigns selective SSM and projection layers of single-layer Mamba, guided by four TSC-specific hypotheses. The authors re-evaluated 20 strong baselines across all 30 UEA datasets under a unified protocol to address benchmarking limitations.
AI.result: MambaSL achieves state-of-the-art performance with statistically significant average improvements. The framework ensures reproducibility via public checkpoints for all evaluated models. Visualizations demonstrate the potential of Mamba-based architectures as a TSC backbone.
AI.conclusion: MambaSL demonstrates that Mamba-based architectures have strong potential as a backbone for time series classification, achieving state-of-the-art results while addressing reproducibility issues in the field through comprehensive benchmarking and public checkpoints.

title: From Reactive to Proactive: Assessing the Proactivity of Voice Agents via ProVoice-Bench
summary: Recent advancements in LLM agents are gradually shifting from reactive, text-based paradigms toward proactive, multimodal interaction. However, existing benchmarks primarily focus on reactive responses, overlooking the complexities of proactive intervention and monitoring. To bridge this gap, we introduce ProVoice-Bench, the first evaluation framework specifically designed for proactive voice agents, featuring four novel tasks. By leveraging a multi-stage data synthesis pipeline, we curate 1,182 high-quality samples for rigorous testing. Our evaluation of state-of-the-art Multimodal LLMs reveals a significant performance gap, particularly regarding over-triggering and reasoning capabilities. These findings highlight the limitations of current models and offer a roadmap for developing more natural, context-aware proactive agents.

AI.tldr: ProVoice-Bench: First evaluation framework for proactive voice agents with four novel tasks, revealing significant performance gaps in current multimodal LLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on reactive responses, overlooking proactive intervention and monitoring capabilities needed for natural voice agent interactions.
AI.method: Developed a multi-stage data synthesis pipeline to curate 1,182 high-quality samples across four novel tasks specifically designed for proactive voice agent evaluation.
AI.result: Evaluation of state-of-the-art Multimodal LLMs shows significant performance gaps, particularly regarding over-triggering and reasoning capabilities in proactive scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Current models have limitations in proactive capabilities; ProVoice-Bench provides a roadmap for developing more natural, context-aware proactive agents.

title: AdaSplash-2: Faster Differentiable Sparse Attention
summary: Sparse attention has been proposed as a way to alleviate the quadratic cost of transformers, a central bottleneck in long-context training. A promising line of work is $α$-entmax attention, a differentiable sparse alternative to softmax that enables input-dependent sparsity yet has lagged behind softmax due to the computational overhead necessary to compute the normalizer $τ$. In this paper, we introduce AdaSplash-2, which addresses this limitation through a novel histogram-based initialization that reduces the number of iterations needed to compute $τ$ to typically 1--2. The key idea is to compute a coarse histogram of attention scores on the fly and store it in on-chip SRAM, yielding a more accurate initialization that enables fast forward and backward computation. Combined with a sparsity-aware GPU implementation that skips zero blocks with low overhead, AdaSplash-2 matches or improves per-step training time relative to FlashAttention-2 when block sparsity is moderate-to-high (e.g., $>$60\%), which often occurs at long-context lengths. On downstream tasks, models trained with our efficient $α$-entmax attention match softmax baselines at short-context lengths and achieve substantial gains in long-context settings.

AI.tldr: AdaSplash-2 is a novel attention mechanism that enables efficient sparse attention computation using a histogram-based initialization to reduce iterations needed for normalizer calculation, matching or improving performance over FlashAttention-2 in moderate-to-high sparsity scenarios.
AI.motivation: Transformers suffer from quadratic cost in long-context training, and while α-entmax attention offers a differentiable sparse alternative to softmax, it has computational overhead from computing the normalizer τ, limiting its adoption despite promising input-dependent sparsity capabilities.
AI.method: AdaSplash-2 uses a histogram-based initialization computed on-the-fly and stored in on-chip SRAM to reduce τ computation iterations to typically 1-2. It combines this with a sparsity-aware GPU implementation that skips zero blocks with low overhead, enabling fast forward and backward computation.
AI.result: AdaSplash-2 matches or improves per-step training time relative to FlashAttention-2 when block sparsity is moderate-to-high (>60%), which often occurs at long-context lengths. Models trained with efficient α-entmax attention match softmax baselines at short contexts and achieve substantial gains in long-context settings.
AI.conclusion: AdaSplash-2 successfully addresses the computational bottleneck of α-entmax attention through histogram-based initialization and optimized GPU implementation, making sparse attention practical for long-context training while maintaining or improving downstream task performance.

title: Where are the Humans? A Scoping Review of Fairness in Multi-agent AI Systems
comment: In proceedings of European Conference on Information Systems (ECIS) 2026
summary: Rapid advances in Generative AI are giving rise to increasingly sophisticated Multi-Agent AI (MAAI) systems. While AI fairness has been extensively studied in traditional predictive scenarios, its examination in MAAI remains nascent and fragmented. This scoping review critically synthesizes existing research on fairness in MAAI systems. Through a qualitative content analysis of 23 selected studies, we identify five archetypal approaches. Our findings reveal that fairness in MAAI systems is often addressed superficially, lacks robust normative foundations, and frequently overlooks the complex dynamics introduced by agent autonomy and system-level interactions. We argue that fairness must be embedded structurally throughout the development lifecycle of MAAI, rather than appended as a post-hoc consideration. Meaningful evaluation requires explicit human oversight, normative clarity, and a precise articulation of fairness objectives and beneficiaries. This review provides a foundation for advancing fairness research in MAAI systems by highlighting critical gaps, exposing prevailing limitations, and suggesting pathways.

AI.tldr: This scoping review synthesizes existing research on fairness in Multi-Agent AI systems, finding current approaches are superficial, lack normative foundations, and overlook agent autonomy dynamics. The authors argue fairness must be embedded structurally throughout MAAI development.
AI.motivation: Rapid advances in Generative AI are leading to sophisticated Multi-Agent AI systems, but fairness research in these systems remains nascent and fragmented. Traditional AI fairness studies have focused on predictive scenarios, but MAAI systems present unique challenges that haven't been adequately addressed.
AI.method: The researchers conducted a scoping review with qualitative content analysis of 23 selected studies on fairness in MAAI systems. They identified five archetypal approaches through this analysis methodology.
AI.result: The review found that fairness in MAAI systems is often addressed superficially, lacks robust normative foundations, and frequently overlooks complex dynamics introduced by agent autonomy and system-level interactions. Current approaches fail to adequately consider these unique aspects of multi-agent systems.
AI.conclusion: Fairness must be embedded structurally throughout the development lifecycle of MAAI rather than being appended as a post-hoc consideration. Meaningful evaluation requires explicit human oversight, normative clarity, and precise articulation of fairness objectives and beneficiaries. The review provides a foundation for advancing fairness research by highlighting gaps and suggesting pathways forward.

title: One-shot learning for the complex dynamical behaviors of weakly nonlinear forced oscillators
summary: Extrapolative prediction of complex nonlinear dynamics remains a central challenge in engineering. This study proposes a one-shot learning method to identify global frequency-response curves from a single excitation time history by learning governing equations. We introduce MEv-SINDy (Multi-frequency Evolutionary Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics) to infer the governing equations of non-autonomous and multi-frequency systems. The methodology leverages the Generalized Harmonic Balance (GHB) method to decompose complex forced responses into a set of slow-varying evolution equations. We validated the capabilities of MEv-SINDy on two critical Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS). These applications include a nonlinear beam resonator and a MEMS micromirror. Our results show that the model trained on a single point accurately predicts softening/hardening effects and jump phenomena across a wide range of excitation levels. This approach significantly reduces the data acquisition burden for the characterization and design of nonlinear microsystems.

AI.tldr: MEv-SINDy enables one-shot learning of global frequency-response curves from a single excitation time history by inferring governing equations for nonlinear dynamic systems.
AI.motivation: Extrapolative prediction of complex nonlinear dynamics in engineering systems remains challenging, requiring extensive data acquisition for characterization and design of nonlinear microsystems like MEMS devices.
AI.method: Proposes MEv-SINDy (Multi-frequency Evolutionary Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics) that combines Generalized Harmonic Balance (GHB) method to decompose forced responses into slow-varying evolution equations, enabling identification of governing equations from a single excitation history.
AI.result: Validated on two MEMS systems (nonlinear beam resonator and MEMS micromirror) showing accurate prediction of softening/hardening effects and jump phenomena across wide excitation ranges using models trained on single data points.
AI.conclusion: The approach significantly reduces data acquisition burden for characterizing nonlinear microsystems, enabling efficient prediction of complex dynamic behaviors from minimal experimental data.

title: OpenMobile: Building Open Mobile Agents with Task and Trajectory Synthesis
summary: Mobile agents powered by vision-language models have demonstrated impressive capabilities in automating mobile tasks, with recent leading models achieving a marked performance leap, e.g., nearly 70% success on AndroidWorld. However, these systems keep their training data closed and remain opaque about their task and trajectory synthesis recipes. We present OpenMobile, an open-source framework that synthesizes high-quality task instructions and agent trajectories, with two key components: (1) The first is a scalable task synthesis pipeline that constructs a global environment memory from exploration, then leverages it to generate diverse and grounded instructions. and (2) a policy-switching strategy for trajectory rollout. By alternating between learner and expert models, it captures essential error-recovery data often missing in standard imitation learning. Agents trained on our data achieve competitive results across three dynamic mobile agent benchmarks: notably, our fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL and Qwen3-VL reach 51.7% and 64.7% on AndroidWorld, far surpassing existing open-data approaches. Furthermore, we conduct transparent analyses on the overlap between our synthetic instructions and benchmark test sets, and verify that performance gains stem from broad functionality coverage rather than benchmark overfitting. We release data and code at https://njucckevin.github.io/openmobile/ to bridge the data gap and facilitate broader mobile agent research.

AI.tldr: OpenMobile is an open-source framework that synthesizes high-quality task instructions and agent trajectories for mobile agents, achieving competitive performance on AndroidWorld while providing transparency and open data to bridge the data gap in mobile agent research.
AI.motivation: Current mobile agent systems powered by vision-language models achieve impressive performance (nearly 70% success on AndroidWorld) but keep their training data closed and opaque about task/trajectory synthesis methods, creating a data gap in the research community.
AI.method: Two key components: (1) A scalable task synthesis pipeline that constructs global environment memory from exploration to generate diverse, grounded instructions; (2) A policy-switching strategy for trajectory rollout that alternates between learner and expert models to capture essential error-recovery data missing in standard imitation learning.
AI.result: Agents trained on OpenMobile data achieve competitive results: fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL reaches 51.7% and Qwen3-VL reaches 64.7% on AndroidWorld, far surpassing existing open-data approaches. Performance gains come from broad functionality coverage rather than benchmark overfitting.
AI.conclusion: OpenMobile provides transparent, open-source framework and data to bridge the data gap in mobile agent research, enabling competitive performance while ensuring transparency about synthetic instruction overlap with benchmark test sets and avoiding benchmark overfitting.

title: RL-STPA: Adapting System-Theoretic Hazard Analysis for Safety-Critical Reinforcement Learning
summary: As reinforcement learning (RL) deployments expand into safety-critical domains, existing evaluation methods fail to systematically identify hazards arising from the black-box nature of neural network enabled policies and distributional shift between training and deployment. This paper introduces Reinforcement Learning System-Theoretic Process Analysis (RL-STPA), a framework that adapts conventional STPA's systematic hazard analysis to address RL's unique challenges through three key contributions: hierarchical subtask decomposition using both temporal phase analysis and domain expertise to capture emergent behaviors, coverage-guided perturbation testing that explores the sensitivity of state-action spaces, and iterative checkpoints that feed identified hazards back into training through reward shaping and curriculum design. We demonstrate RL-STPA in the safety-critical test case of autonomous drone navigation and landing, revealing potential loss scenarios that can be missed by standard RL evaluations. The proposed framework provides practitioners with a toolkit for systematic hazard analysis, quantitative metrics for safety coverage assessment, and actionable guidelines for establishing operational safety bounds. While RL-STPA cannot provide formal guarantees for arbitrary neural policies, it offers a practical methodology for systematically evaluating and improving RL safety and robustness in safety-critical applications where exhaustive verification methods remain intractable.

AI.tldr: RL-STPA is a safety analysis framework that adapts system-theoretic process analysis to systematically identify hazards in RL systems, particularly for safety-critical applications like autonomous drones.
AI.motivation: Existing RL evaluation methods fail to systematically identify hazards arising from neural network black-box policies and distributional shift between training and deployment in safety-critical domains.
AI.method: Three key contributions: 1) hierarchical subtask decomposition using temporal phase analysis and domain expertise, 2) coverage-guided perturbation testing of state-action spaces, and 3) iterative checkpoints feeding identified hazards back into training through reward shaping and curriculum design.
AI.result: Demonstrated in autonomous drone navigation and landing, revealing potential loss scenarios missed by standard RL evaluations. Provides practitioners with systematic hazard analysis toolkit, quantitative safety coverage metrics, and actionable operational safety guidelines.
AI.conclusion: While RL-STPA cannot provide formal guarantees for arbitrary neural policies, it offers a practical methodology for systematically evaluating and improving RL safety and robustness in safety-critical applications where exhaustive verification remains intractable.

title: HyperSpace: A Generalized Framework for Spatial Encoding in Hyperdimensional Representations
summary: Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) provide a well-defined algebraic framework for compositional representations in hyperdimensional spaces. We introduce HyperSpace, an open-source framework that decomposes VSA systems into modular operators for encoding, binding, bundling, similarity, cleanup, and regression. Using HyperSpace, we analyze and benchmark two representative VSA backends: Holographic Reduced Representations (HRR) and Fourier Holographic Reduced Representations (FHRR). Although FHRR provides lower theoretical complexity for individual operations, HyperSpaces modularity reveals that similarity and cleanup dominate runtime in spatial domains. As a result, HRR and FHRR exhibit comparable end-to-end performance. Differences in memory footprint introduce additional deployment trade-offs where HRR requires approximately half the memory of FHRR vectors. By enabling modular, system-level evaluation, HyperSpace reveals practical trade-offs in VSA pipelines that are not apparent from theoretical or operator-level comparisons alone.

AI.tldr: HyperSpace is an open-source framework that decomposes VSA systems into modular operators, enabling practical benchmarking that reveals HRR and FHRR have comparable end-to-end performance despite FHRR's lower theoretical complexity, with HRR having memory advantages.
AI.motivation: To enable modular, system-level evaluation of Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) that reveals practical trade-offs not apparent from theoretical or operator-level comparisons alone.
AI.method: Develop HyperSpace, an open-source framework that decomposes VSA systems into modular operators (encoding, binding, bundling, similarity, cleanup, regression), then analyze and benchmark two representative VSA backends: Holographic Reduced Representations (HRR) and Fourier Holographic Reduced Representations (FHRR).
AI.result: Although FHRR has lower theoretical complexity for individual operations, similarity and cleanup operations dominate runtime in spatial domains, making HRR and FHRR comparable in end-to-end performance. HRR requires approximately half the memory of FHRR vectors, introducing additional deployment trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: HyperSpace's modular approach reveals practical trade-offs in VSA pipelines that theoretical analyses miss, showing that system-level evaluation is crucial for understanding real-world VSA performance and that memory considerations can outweigh theoretical complexity advantages.

title: Optimal last-iterate convergence in matrix games with bandit feedback using the log-barrier
summary: We study the problem of learning minimax policies in zero-sum matrix games. Fiegel et al. (2025) recently showed that achieving last-iterate convergence in this setting is harder when the players are uncoupled, by proving a lower bound on the exploitability gap of Omega(t^{-1/4}). Some online mirror descent algorithms were proposed in the literature for this problem, but none have truly attained this rate yet. We show that the use of a log-barrier regularization, along with a dual-focused analysis, allows this O-tilde(t^{-1/4}) convergence with high-probability. We additionally extend our idea to the setting of extensive-form games, proving a bound with the same rate.

AI.tldr: Proposes using log-barrier regularization with dual-focused analysis to achieve Õ(t^{-1/4}) convergence for minimax policies in zero-sum matrix games, matching the known lower bound, and extends this to extensive-form games.
AI.motivation: Recent work showed achieving last-iterate convergence in zero-sum games is harder for uncoupled players, with a lower bound of Ω(t^{-1/4}) on exploitability gap. Existing online mirror descent algorithms haven't achieved this rate yet, creating a gap between lower bound and practical algorithms.
AI.method: Uses log-barrier regularization combined with a dual-focused analysis approach. This technique is applied to the problem of learning minimax policies in zero-sum matrix games and is subsequently extended to extensive-form games.
AI.result: Achieves Õ(t^{-1/4}) convergence rate with high probability for zero-sum matrix games, matching the theoretical lower bound. Successfully extends the same convergence rate to extensive-form games as well.
AI.conclusion: Log-barrier regularization with dual-focused analysis enables achieving the optimal Õ(t^{-1/4}) convergence rate for learning minimax policies in zero-sum games, bridging the gap between theoretical lower bounds and practical algorithms, with applicability to both matrix and extensive-form games.

title: SRMU: Relevance-Gated Updates for Streaming Hyperdimensional Memories
summary: Sequential associative memories (SAMs) are difficult to build and maintain in real-world streaming environments, where observations arrive incrementally over time, have imbalanced sampling, and non-stationary temporal dynamics. Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) provide a biologically-inspired framework for building SAMs. Entities and attributes are encoded as quasi-orthogonal hyperdimensional vectors and processed with well defined algebraic operations. Despite this rich framework, most VSA systems rely on simple additive updates, where repeated observations reinforce existing information even when no new information is introduced. In non-stationary environments, this leads to the persistence of stale information after the underlying system changes. In this work, we introduce the Sequential Relevance Memory Unit (SRMU), a domain- and cleanup-agnostic update rule for VSA-based SAMs. The SRMU combines temporal decay with a relevance gating mechanism. Unlike prior approaches that solely rely on cleanup, the SRMU regulates memory formation by filtering redundant, conflicting, and stale information before storage. We evaluate the SRMU on streaming state-tracking tasks that isolate non-uniform sampling and non-stationary temporal dynamics. Our results show that the SRMU increases memory similarity by $12.6\%$ and reduces cumulative memory magnitude by $53.5\%$. This shows that the SRMU produces more stable memory growth and stronger alignment with the ground-truth state.

AI.tldr: SRMU is a new update rule for Vector Symbolic Architecture-based sequential associative memories that uses temporal decay and relevance gating to filter redundant, conflicting, and stale information in non-stationary streaming environments.
AI.motivation: Traditional VSA systems use simple additive updates that reinforce existing information even when no new information is introduced, leading to persistence of stale information in non-stationary environments with imbalanced sampling and changing temporal dynamics.
AI.method: Proposes Sequential Relevance Memory Unit (SRMU), a domain- and cleanup-agnostic update rule combining temporal decay with relevance gating mechanism to regulate memory formation by filtering redundant, conflicting, and stale information before storage.
AI.result: On streaming state-tracking tasks, SRMU increases memory similarity by 12.6% and reduces cumulative memory magnitude by 53.5%, producing more stable memory growth and stronger alignment with ground-truth state.
AI.conclusion: SRMU effectively addresses limitations of traditional VSA systems in non-stationary streaming environments by filtering outdated information before storage, leading to more stable and accurate sequential associative memories.

title: Stability and Generalization in Looped Transformers
summary: Looped transformers promise test-time compute scaling by spending more iterations on harder problems, but it remains unclear which architectural choices let them extrapolate to harder problems at test time rather than memorize training-specific solutions. We introduce a fixed-point based framework for analyzing looped architectures along three axes of stability -- reachability, input-dependence, and geometry -- and use it to characterize when fixed-point iteration yields meaningful predictions. Theoretically, we prove that looped networks without recall have countable fixed points and cannot achieve strong input-dependence at any spectral regime, while recall combined with outer normalization reliably produces a regime in which fixed points are simultaneously reachable, locally smooth in the input, and supported by stable backpropagation. Empirically, we train single-layer looped transformers on chess, sudoku, and prefix-sums and find that downstream performance tracks the framework's predictions across tasks and architectural configurations. We additionally introduce internal recall, a novel recall placement variant, and show that it becomes competitive with -- and on sudoku, substantially better than -- standard recall placement once outer normalization is applied.

AI.tldr: Looped transformers need specific architectural features (recall + outer normalization) to properly extrapolate to harder problems rather than memorize training data, with a new internal recall variant showing strong performance on certain tasks.
AI.motivation: Looped transformers promise test-time compute scaling by spending more iterations on harder problems, but current architectures often fail to properly extrapolate to harder problems and instead memorize training-specific solutions. The researchers want to understand what architectural choices enable meaningful extrapolation.
AI.method: Introduced a fixed-point based framework analyzing looped architectures along three stability axes: reachability, input-dependence, and geometry. Theoretically analyzed different architectural configurations, then empirically validated by training single-layer looped transformers on chess, sudoku, and prefix-sum tasks.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows looped networks without recall have countable fixed points and cannot achieve strong input-dependence. Recall combined with outer normalization produces stable, reachable fixed points that are locally smooth in inputs. Empirically, downstream performance tracks framework predictions across tasks. Introduced internal recall variant which becomes competitive/strong with outer normalization.
AI.conclusion: Proper architectural design (specifically recall combined with outer normalization) enables looped transformers to meaningfully extrapolate to harder problems rather than memorize training data. The proposed framework successfully predicts performance, and internal recall represents a promising architectural variant.

title: An Axiomatic Benchmark for Evaluation of Scientific Novelty Metrics
summary: The rigorous evaluation of the novelty of a scientific paper is, even for human scientists, a challenging task. With the increasing interest in AI scientists and AI involvement in scientific idea generation and paper writing, it also becomes increasingly important that this task be automatable and reliable, lest both human attention and compute tokens be wasted on ideas that have already been explored. Due to the challenge of quantifying ground-truth novelty, however, existing novelty metrics for scientific papers generally validate their results against noisy, confounded signals such as citation counts or peer review scores. These proxies can conflate novelty with impact, quality, or reviewer preference, which in turn makes it harder to assess how well a given metric actually evaluates novelty. We therefore propose an axiomatic benchmark for scientific novelty metrics. We first define a set of axioms that a well-behaved novelty metric should satisfy, grounded in human scientific norms and practice, then evaluate existing metrics across ten tasks spanning three domains of AI research. Our results reveal that no existing metric satisfies all axioms consistently, and that metrics fail on systematically different axioms, reflecting their underlying architectures. Additionally, we show that combining metrics of complementary architectures leads to consistent improvements on the benchmark, with per-axiom weighting achieving 90.1% versus 71.5% for the best individual metric, suggesting that developing architecturally diverse metrics is a promising direction for future work. We release the benchmark code as supplementary material to encourage the development of more robust scientific literature novelty metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an axiomatic benchmark for evaluating scientific novelty metrics, revealing that no existing metric satisfies all axioms, and shows that combining complementary metrics improves performance.
AI.motivation: Evaluating scientific paper novelty is challenging even for humans, and with increasing AI involvement in scientific idea generation, reliable automated novelty assessment becomes crucial to avoid wasting resources on already-explored ideas. Existing metrics use noisy proxies like citation counts that conflate novelty with impact/quality.
AI.method: Proposes an axiomatic benchmark: (1) Defines axioms for well-behaved novelty metrics based on human scientific norms, (2) Evaluates existing metrics across 10 tasks spanning 3 AI research domains, (3) Tests metric combinations with per-axiom weighting.
AI.result: No existing metric satisfies all axioms consistently; metrics fail on systematically different axioms reflecting their architectures. Combining complementary metrics with per-axiom weighting achieves 90.1% vs 71.5% for best individual metric.
AI.conclusion: Developing architecturally diverse novelty metrics is promising. The released benchmark code encourages development of more robust scientific literature novelty metrics.

title: How Embeddings Shape Graph Neural Networks: Classical vs Quantum-Oriented Node Representations
summary: Node embeddings act as the information interface for graph neural networks, yet their empirical impact is often reported under mismatched backbones, splits, and training budgets. This paper provides a controlled benchmark of embedding choices for graph classification, comparing classical baselines with quantum-oriented node representations under a unified pipeline. We evaluate two classical baselines alongside quantum-oriented alternatives, including a circuit-defined variational embedding and quantum-inspired embeddings computed via graph operators and linear-algebraic constructions. All variants are trained and tested with the same backbone, stratified splits, identical optimization and early stopping, and consistent metrics. Experiments on five different TU datasets and on QM9 converted to classification via target binning show clear dataset dependence: quantum-oriented embeddings yield the most consistent gains on structure-driven benchmarks, while social graphs with limited node attributes remain well served by classical baselines. The study highlights practical trade-offs between inductive bias, trainability, and stability under a fixed training budget, and offers a reproducible reference point for selecting quantum-oriented embeddings in graph learning.

AI.tldr: A controlled benchmark study comparing classical vs quantum-oriented node embeddings for graph classification across multiple datasets reveals dataset-dependent performance with quantum embeddings excelling on structure-driven benchmarks while classical embeddings work well for social graphs with limited node attributes.
AI.motivation: Previous empirical evaluations of node embeddings for graph neural networks suffer from inconsistencies in backbones, data splits, and training budgets, making fair comparisons difficult. The paper aims to provide a controlled benchmark to properly assess the impact of different embedding choices, particularly comparing classical baselines with emerging quantum-oriented alternatives.
AI.method: Established a unified experimental pipeline comparing two classical baselines against quantum-oriented alternatives including circuit-defined variational embeddings and quantum-inspired embeddings computed via graph operators and linear-algebraic constructions. All variants were evaluated under identical conditions: same backbone architecture, stratified data splits, identical optimization procedures, early stopping, and consistent metrics across five TU datasets and QM9 (converted to classification via target binning).
AI.result: Clear dataset dependence emerged: quantum-oriented embeddings achieved the most consistent performance gains on structure-driven benchmarks, while social graphs with limited node attributes remained well-served by classical baselines. The study reveals practical trade-offs between inductive bias, trainability, and stability under fixed training budgets.
AI.conclusion: Quantum-oriented embeddings show promise particularly for structure-driven graph classification tasks, but their effectiveness is dataset-dependent. The study provides a reproducible reference point for selecting quantum-oriented embeddings in graph learning and highlights the importance of controlled benchmarking for fair comparisons of embedding techniques.

title: IG-Search: Step-Level Information Gain Rewards for Search-Augmented Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning has emerged as an effective paradigm for training large language models to perform search-augmented reasoning. However, existing approaches rely on trajectory-level rewards that cannot distinguish precise search queries from vague or redundant ones within a rollout group, and collapse to a near-zero gradient signal whenever every sampled trajectory fails. In this paper, we propose IG-Search, a reinforcement learning framework that introduces a step-level reward based on Information Gain (IG). For each search step, IG measures how much the retrieved documents improve the model's confidence in the gold answer relative to a counterfactual baseline of random documents, thereby reflecting the effectiveness of the underlying search query. This signal is fed back to the corresponding search-query tokens via per-token advantage modulation in GRPO, enabling fine-grained, step-level credit assignment within a rollout. Unlike prior step-level methods that require either externally annotated intermediate supervision or shared environment states across trajectories, IG-Search derives its signals from the policy's own generation probabilities, requiring no intermediate annotations beyond standard question-answer pairs. Experiments on seven single-hop and multi-hop QA benchmarks demonstrate that IG-Search achieves an average EM of 0.430 with Qwen2.5-3B, outperforming the strongest trajectory-level baseline (MR-Search) by 1.6 points and the step-level method GiGPO by 0.9 points on average across benchmarks, with particularly pronounced gains on multi-hop reasoning tasks. Despite introducing a dense step-level signal, IG-Search adds only ~6.4% to per-step training wall-clock time over the trajectory-level baseline and leaves inference latency unchanged, while still providing a meaningful gradient signal even when every sampled trajectory answers incorrectly.

AI.tldr: IG-Search is a reinforcement learning framework that uses Information Gain-based step-level rewards to train LLMs for search-augmented reasoning, outperforming trajectory-level methods with fine-grained credit assignment and better multi-hop performance.
AI.motivation: Existing RL approaches for search-augmented reasoning suffer from two key limitations: (1) trajectory-level rewards cannot distinguish effective vs. ineffective search queries within rollout groups, and (2) they provide near-zero gradient signals when all sampled trajectories fail to answer correctly.
AI.method: IG-Search introduces step-level rewards based on Information Gain (IG), which measures how much retrieved documents improve the model's confidence in the correct answer compared to random documents. This IG signal is fed back to corresponding search-query tokens via per-token advantage modulation in GRPO, enabling fine-grained credit assignment. Unlike prior methods, it requires no intermediate annotations beyond standard QA pairs.
AI.result: On seven QA benchmarks, IG-Search achieves an average EM of 0.430 with Qwen2.5-3B, outperforming the strongest trajectory-level baseline (MR-Search) by 1.6 points and step-level method GiGPO by 0.9 points.
